content.summary:
User asked: "https://github.com/earendil-works/pi inspect this and tell me if we can achieve our existing opencode setup with hooks etc in pi... I want a gap analysis, implementation effort doesn't need to be considered". Plan mode is active, so the entire task is read-only: inspect, analyze, and produce a gap analysis only; no edits, no installs, no file/system modifications. The `gap-analysis` skill was loaded and says to compare external resource features to current repo state, build a feature inventory/current-state map, classify Present/Partial/Missing/N/A, rank opportunities by value score, and report. User explicitly said implementation effort does not need to be considered, so feasibility/effort should not drive conclusions; focus on whether Pi can achieve parity and what gaps remain.

External repo inspected remotely: `https://github.com/earendil-works/pi`. README summary: Pi is an AI agent harness monorepo with packages `@earendil-works/pi-coding-agent` (interactive coding agent CLI), `@earendil-works/pi-agent-core` (agent runtime with tool calling/state), `@earendil-works/pi-ai` (multi-provider LLM API), plus TUI/web UI packages. Top-level tree includes `.pi/extensions`, `.pi/prompts`, `packages/agent`, `packages/coding-agent`, etc. No local clone was made.

Pi coding-agent README findings:
- Pi is a minimal terminal coding harness designed to be extended with TypeScript extensions, skills, prompt templates, themes, and Pi packages.
- Modes: interactive, print/JSON, RPC over JSONL, and SDK embedding.
- Default built-in tools include `read`, `write`, `edit`, `bash`, plus CLI ref later lists `grep`, `find`, `ls`.
- Providers/models: built-in provider registry with subscription auth for Anthropic Claude Pro/Max, OpenAI ChatGPT Plus/Pro/Codex, GitHub Copilot; API-key providers include Anthropic, OpenAI, Azure OpenAI, DeepSeek, Google Gemini/Vertex, Bedrock, Mistral, Groq, Cerebras, Cloudflare, xAI, OpenRouter, Vercel AI Gateway, ZAI, OpenCode Zen/Go, Hugging Face, Fireworks, Together, Kimi, MiniMax, Xiaomi. Custom providers via `~/.pi/agent/models.json` if they speak OpenAI/Anthropic/Google APIs; deeper custom APIs/OAuth via extensions.
- Interactive features: startup header lists loaded `AGENTS.md`, prompt templates, skills, extensions; editor supports `@` file references, path completion, multiline input, images, and user bash commands with `!`/`!!`.
- Commands include `/login`, `/logout`, `/model`, `/settings`, `/resume`, `/new`, `/name`, `/session`, `/tree`, `/fork`, `/clone`, `/compact`, `/copy`, `/export`, `/share`, `/reload`, `/hotkeys`, `/changelog`, `/quit`.
- Queue model: Enter during work queues steering message; Alt+Enter queues follow-up; Escape aborts; settings support `steeringMode` and `followUpMode` as `one-at-a-time` or `all`.
- Sessions are JSONL tree-structured files under `~/.pi/agent/sessions/`; support continue/resume, no-session, specific session, fork/clone, `/tree` branch navigation with labels/bookmarks, and compaction.
- Compaction: manual `/compact [prompt]`, automatic on overflow/threshold, full history remains in JSONL, extension-customizable.
- Settings: global `~/.pi/agent/settings.json`, project `.pi/settings.json`. Includes model, thinking, UI, compaction, retry, message delivery, terminal/images, shell, sessionDir, enabledModels, markdown, packages/extensions/skills/prompts/themes, `enableSkillCommands`. Startup update/telemetry can be disabled by `PI_SKIP_VERSION_CHECK`, `PI_TELEMETRY=0`, `PI_OFFLINE=1`.
- Context files: Pi loads `AGENTS.md` or `CLAUDE.md` at startup from `~/.pi/agent/AGENTS.md`, parent dirs upward from cwd, and current dir; can disable with `--no-context-files`. `.pi/SYSTEM.md` or `~/.pi/agent/SYSTEM.md` can replace system prompt; `APPEND_SYSTEM.md` appends.
- Customization: prompt templates as markdown under `~/.pi/agent/prompts/`, `.pi/prompts/`, packages, settings, or CLI; Skills implement Agent Skills standard; Extensions are TypeScript modules under `~/.pi/agent/extensions/`, `.pi/extensions/`, or packages.
- Pi package system bundles extensions/skills/prompts/themes via npm/git; installed with `pi install`, listed/updated/configured. Security warning: extensions run arbitrary code, skills can induce arbitrary actions.
- Philosophy: core intentionally omits MCP, sub-agents, plan mode, permission popups, built-in todos, and background bash; those are meant to be built by extensions/packages, use tmux/container, or external tools.

Pi agent-core README findings:
- `Agent` supports stateful tool calling, event streaming, custom message types, context transformation, provider stream functions, session IDs, dynamic API key resolution, `toolExecution` parallel/sequential, `beforeToolCall` and `afterToolCall` hooks, `shouldStopAfterTurn`, steering/follow-up queues, abort/waitForIdle.
- Tool lifecycle includes events: `agent_start`, `turn_start`, `message_start`, `message_update`, `message_end`, `tool_execution_start/update/end`, `turn_end`, `agent_end`.
- Hooks: `beforeToolCall` can block execution after args validation; `afterToolCall` can patch/terminate result. Tools can `terminate: true` to skip follow-up if all finalized tool results terminate.
- Supports custom messages via declaration merging and `convertToLlm` filtering.
- `AgentTool` schema includes `name`, `label`, `description`, `parameters`, optional `executionMode`, `execute` with streaming updates.

Pi `AgentHarness` docs (`packages/agent/docs/agent-harness.md`) findings:
- Harness owns session persistence, runtime config, resources, operation locking, and extension-facing semantics. It is still partly migration-oriented with todos.
- State split: harness config, turn snapshot, persisted session, pending session writes.
- Phase model: `idle`, `turn`, `compaction`, `branch_summary`, `retry`. Structural operations (`prompt`, `skill`, `promptFromTemplate`, `compact`, `navigateTree`) require idle. Queue ops (`steer`, `followUp`, `nextTurn`), abort, and runtime setters are allowed during turns.
- Save points flush pending session writes after agent-emitted messages, refresh turn state before next provider request, and let model/thinking/tools/resources/stream/system prompt changes affect later turns.
- Current hooks/listeners receive only payloads in core harness; future extension context/facade/session facade are planned to avoid deadlocks and ordered writes.
- Provider hooks are implemented in harness: `before_provider_request` can patch stream options; `before_provider_payload` can inspect/replace provider-specific payloads; `after_provider_response` observes status/headers before body consumption.
- Tool registry: set tools/active tools exists; getter semantics and update observability events remain TODO.
- Lifecycle TODOs include finalizing phase/settled semantics, auto-compaction decision point, retry handling, before_agent_start semantics, abort audit, model registry design, generic hook/event extension mechanism, converting remaining throw paths to typed results.

Pi `packages/agent/src/harness/types.ts` findings:
- Defines `Skill` (name, description, content, filePath, `disableModelInvocation`), `PromptTemplate`, `AgentHarnessResources`, `AgentHarnessStreamOptions`, `ExecutionEnv`/`FileSystem`/`Shell`, session entry types, event types, hook result maps, compaction/tree types, and `AgentHarnessOptions`.
- Events include `queue_update`, `save_point`, `abort`, `settled`, `before_agent_start`, `context`, `before_provider_request`, `before_provider_payload`, `after_provider_response`, `tool_call`, `tool_result`, `session_before_compact`, `session_compact`, `session_before_tree`, `session_tree`, `model_select`, `thinking_level_select`, `resources_update`, plus low-level `AgentEvent`.
- Hook results include `BeforeAgentStartResult` (`messages?`, `systemPrompt?`), `ContextResult`, `BeforeProviderRequestResult`, `BeforeProviderPayloadResult`, `ToolCallResult` (`block?`, `reason?`), `ToolResultPatch`, `SessionBeforeCompactResult`, `SessionBeforeTreeResult`.

Pi skill docs and implementation findings:
- Skills follow Agent Skills standard and load from global `~/.pi/agent/skills/` and `~/.agents/skills/`, project `.pi/skills/` and `.agents/skills/` in cwd/ancestors, packages, settings, and CLI `--skill`.
- Skills can be invoked via `/skill:name`; full `SKILL.md` loads on demand. `disable-model-invocation` hides skill from model-visible list while explicit invocation still works.
- `skills.ts` recursively loads `SKILL.md` directories, root `.md` files for some locations, honors `.gitignore`/`.ignore`/`.fdignore`, validates name/description, requires description, and formats skill invocation as XML with relative-reference guidance.
- Pi can reuse Claude Code or Codex skill dirs by adding them to settings, e.g. `"skills": ["~/.claude/skills", "~/.codex/skills"]`; for project `.claude/skills`, add `"skills": ["../.claude/skills"]` in `.pi/settings.json`.

Pi extensions docs findings (long file was truncated but useful parts captured):
- Extensions are TypeScript modules loaded by jiti. Locations: `~/.pi/agent/extensions/*.ts`, `~/.pi/agent/extensions/*/index.ts`, `.pi/extensions/*.ts`, `.pi/extensions/*/index.ts`, settings `extensions`, CLI `-e/--extension`. `/reload` hot-reloads auto-discovered extensions.
- Extensions can register custom tools, event handlers, commands, shortcuts, flags, custom message renderers, custom UI components, providers, session state, etc.
- Core extension example shows `pi.on("tool_call")` blocking dangerous bash, `pi.registerTool`, `pi.registerCommand`.
- Event lifecycle includes `session_start`, `resources_discover`, `input`, `before_agent_start`, `agent_start`, `turn_start`, `context`, `before_provider_request`, `after_provider_response`, `tool_execution_start`, `tool_call`, `tool_execution_update`, `tool_result`, `tool_execution_end`, `turn_end`, `agent_end`, session switch/fork/compact/tree/shutdown events, model/thinking selection.
- `resources_discover` can contribute `skillPaths`, `promptPaths`, `themePaths`.
- `before_agent_start` can inject a persistent custom message and/or modify chained system prompt; event exposes structured `systemPromptOptions` including customPrompt, selectedTools, toolSnippets, promptGuidelines, cwd, contextFiles, skills.
- `context` can modify messages before each LLM call.
- `before_provider_request` in coding-agent docs appears as provider payload rewrite (note slight mismatch with agent-core harness docs where `before_provider_request` patches stream options and `before_provider_payload` handles provider payload; coding-agent docs may reflect higher-level API).
- `tool_call`: fires after `tool_execution_start` and before tool execution; can block, mutate `event.input` in place, sequential preflight before concurrent sibling execution; no re-validation after mutation.

- `pi.registerTool` supports custom tools with `promptSnippet`, `promptGuidelines`, `prepareArguments`, custom rendering; tools can dynamically register after startup and `pi.setActiveTools()` can enable/disable tools.
- `pi.sendMessage` injects custom messages with `deliverAs: steer|followUp|nextTurn` and optional `triggerTurn`; `pi.sendUserMessage` sends real user messages; `pi.appendEntry` persists extension state not included in LLM context; `pi.setLabel`, `setSessionName`, `registerCommand`, `getCommands`, `registerShortcut`, `registerFlag` exist. Pi prompt/session/RPC docs findings:
- Prompt templates support frontmatter `description` and `argument-hint`, positional args `$1`, `$@`, `${@:N}`, `${@:N:L}`; non-recursive discovery in prompt dirs.
- Session file format v3: JSONL header plus tree entries. Entry types include `message`, `model_change`, `thinking_level_change`, `compaction`, `branch_summary`, `custom`, `custom_message`, `label`, `session_info`. Context build walks branch to root; compaction and branch summaries become messages.
- `SessionManager` API supports create/open/continue/fork/inMemory/list/listAll, append messages/custom entries/labels/session info, branch/tree, context/stats getters.
- RPC mode over strict LF-delimited JSONL supports commands for prompt, steer, follow_up, abort, new_session, get_state, get_messages, set/cycle/list models, set/cycle thinking, set queue modes, compact, auto-compaction/retry, bash/abort_bash, session stats/export/switch/fork/clone/commands. Streams events including agent/message/tool/queue/compaction/retry/extension_error. RPC includes extension UI subprotocol for select/confirm/input/editor/notify/status/widget/title/editor text. - `.config/opencode/plugin/claude-compat.ts` lines 1-260 read partially: bridges Claude hook scripts into OpenCode plugin events. It imports `existsSync`, `mkdtempSync`, `rmSync`, `writeFileSync`, `tmpdir`, `join`, OpenCode `Plugin`. Maps OpenCode tool names to Claude hook names (`bash` -> `Bash`, `read` -> `Read`, `write` -> `Write`, `edit` -> `Edit`, `multiedit` -> `MultiEdit`, patch variants -> `ApplyPatch`, `grep` -> `Grep`, `glob` -> `Glob`). `isWriteTool` detects write/edit/multiedit/patch/apply_patch/apply-patch. It has helpers `runScript`, `runCommand`, sync variants using `Bun.spawn`/`spawnSync`, setting env `CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR`, `PROJECT_ROOT`, `REPO_ROOT`, `DOTFILES_ROOT`, passing JSON payload on stdin. `buildToolPayload()` normalizes `filePath`/`file_path`/`path` into both fields and includes session id/output. `maybeBlock()` throws on nonzero hook result. Potential high-level conclusion from evidence so far: Pi can likely reproduce most OpenCode/Claude hook behavior through TypeScript extensions because it has richer first-class event hooks than OpenCode, including tool pre/post, context injection, session lifecycle, compaction/tree, provider request/response, custom commands/tools/UI, and skill/prompt discovery. Main gaps to analyze carefully: Pi lacks MCP by design (requires extension or CLI/skill replacement), lacks built-in subagents/plan mode/todo/permission popups (requires extensions or external tmux/worktree orchestration), lacks OpenCode-specific DCP/pty/vibeguard plugins unless equivalent Pi extensions are written, and existing OpenCode hook bridge currently reuses Claude hook scripts with Claude-shaped JSON payloads; Pi could call the same scripts but payload/event name adaptation must be mapped. Because user said effort does not matter, focus final report on feasibility/parity, not cost.

284: export const OpenAIRotatePlugin: Plugin = async ({ client, directory }) => {
285:   const pendingPrompts = new Map<string, PromptState>()
286:   const handledMessages = new Set<string>()
287:   const rotatingSessions = new Set<string>()
288: 
289:   async function showToast(message: string, variant: "info" | "success" | "warning" | "error") {
290:     try {
291:       await client.tui.showToast({
292:         query: { directory },
293:         body: {
294:           title: "OpenAI rotation",
295:           message,
296:           variant,
297:           duration: 6000,
298:         },
299:       })
300:     } catch {
301:       // Headless runs or unavailable TUI endpoints are fine.
302:     }
303:   }
304: 
305:   async function rotateAccount(): Promise<RotationResult> {
306:     await debugLog(`rotateAccount authFile=${AUTH_FILE}`)
307:     const auth = await readJsonFile<AuthFile>(AUTH_FILE)
308:     if (!auth?.openai) {
309:       return { ok: false, reason: "No active OpenAI auth found" }
310:     }
311: 
312:     await mkdir(ACCOUNTS_DIR, { recursive: true })
313:     await debugLog(`rotateAccount accountsDir=${ACCOUNTS_DIR}`)
314: 
315:     const accountNames = await ensureCurrentAccountSaved(auth.openai, await readAccountNames())
316:     await debugLog(`rotateAccount names=${accountNames.join(",")}`)
317:     if (accountNames.length === 0) {
318:       return { ok: false, reason: "No saved OpenAI accounts are available" }
319:     }
320: 
321:     const currentKey = authKey(auth.openai)
322: 
323:     let activeName: string | null = null
324:     for (const name of accountNames) {
325:       const saved = await readJsonFile<OpenAIAuth>(join(ACCOUNTS_DIR, name, "openai-auth.json"))
326:       if (authKey(saved) === currentKey) {
327:         activeName = name
328:         await debugLog(`rotateAccount active=${name}`)
329:         break
330:       }
331:     }
332: 
333:     for (const name of rotateFrom(accountNames, activeName)) {
334:       let saved = await readJsonFile<OpenAIAuth & { refresh?: string }>(join(ACCOUNTS_DIR, name, "openai-auth.json"))
335:       if (!saved?.access || authKey(saved) === currentKey) {
336:         await debugLog(`rotateAccount skip=${name}`)
337:         continue
338:       }
339: 
340:       // Check for expiration
341:       const now = Date.now()
342:       if (saved.expires && now > saved.expires && saved.refresh) {
343:         await debugLog(`rotateAccount expired=${name} attempting refresh`)
344:         const refreshed = await refreshProfile(name, saved.refresh)
345:         if (refreshed) {
346:           await writeJsonFile(join(ACCOUNTS_DIR, name, "openai-auth.json"), refreshed)
347:           // Sync to codex via shell command (since we can't easily import the fish function here)
348:           const proc = Bun.spawn(["fish", "-c", `_ai_accounts_sync to-codex "${name}" "${join(ACCOUNTS_DIR, name, "openai-auth.json")}"`], {
349:             stdout: "ignore",
350:             stderr: "ignore",
351:           })
352:           await proc.exited
353:           saved = refreshed
354:         }
355:       }
356: 
357:       let status = await probeToken(saved.access)
358:       await debugLog(`rotateAccount probe=${name} status=${status}`)
359: 
360:       // If probe failed due to auth (401/403) and we have a refresh token, try refreshing once
361:       if (status === 2 && saved.refresh) {
362:         await debugLog(`rotateAccount probe auth failed for ${name}, attempting refresh`)
363:         const refreshed = await refreshProfile(name, saved.refresh)
364:         if (refreshed) {
365:           await writeJsonFile(join(ACCOUNTS_DIR, name, "openai-auth.json"), refreshed)
366:           const proc = Bun.spawn(["fish", "-c", `_ai_accounts_sync to-codex "${name}" "${join(ACCOUNTS_DIR, name, "openai-auth.json")}"`], {
367:             stdout: "ignore",
368:             stderr: "ignore",
369:           })
370:           await proc.exited
371:           saved = refreshed
372:           status = await probeToken(saved.access)
373:           await debugLog(`rotateAccount probe after refresh status=${status}`)
374:         }
375:       }
376: 
377:       if (status !== 0) {
378:         continue
379:       }
380: 
381:       await switchAccount(name, saved)
382:       await debugLog(`rotateAccount switched=${name}`)
383:       return { ok: true, name }
384:     }
385: 
386:     return { ok: false, reason: "All saved OpenAI accounts are currently unavailable" }
387:   }
388: 
389:   function buildReplayBody(pending: PromptState) {
390:     return {
391:       parts: toPromptParts(pending.parts),
392:       ...(pending.agent ? { agent: pending.agent } : {}),

394:       ...(pending.format ? { format: pending.format } : {}),

419:       handledMessages.delete(output.message.id)

447:       if (!pending || !isOpenAIProvider(pending.model.providerID)) {
448:         return
449:       }
450:

454:

Instructions from: /Users/shahe.islam/dotfiles/.claude/AGENTS.md
# AGENTS.md - Claude Code Agent System Reference

Specialized agent system for Claude Code with domain, project-specific, and compact-output subagent files.

## Shell Reminder

- Fish is the primary shell for this machine.
- Do not suggest `unset` for interactive shell usage; use `set -e VAR` in Fish.
- `env -u VAR command` is acceptable for one-off commands started from Fish.
- If an example requires Bash or Zsh syntax, label it explicitly.

## Overview

Each agent is a **Claude Code subagent file** (`.claude/agents/*.md`) with YAML frontmatter defining name, description, tool access, model, and optional features. Claude auto-delegates to subagents based on their description fields. Each subagent runs in its own context window with a custom system prompt.

**Core Features**:
- **Subagent Files**: Markdown files with YAML frontmatter in `.claude/agents/`
- **Auto-Delegation**: Claude uses description fields to decide when to delegate
- **Tool Restrictions**: Each agent has specific tool access (read-only vs full)
- **Model Selection**: `haiku` for fast read-only agents, `inherit` for full capability
- **Persistent Memory**: `memory: project` on architect for cross-session learning
- **Background Mode**: `background: true` on test-runner for concurrent execution
- **Cost Control**: `maxTurns` on bounded agents (test-runner, dotfiles-doctor, mentor)
- **Skill Preloading**: `skills` field injects skill content at startup (shell-expert)
- **MCP Access**: `mcpServers: deepwiki` on architect and mentor for repo documentation
- **Lifecycle Hooks**: SubagentStart/SubagentStop events in settings.json for logging
- **Manual Override**: Use `--persona-[name]` flags for explicit control
- **Flag Integration**: Works with all thinking flags, MCP servers, and command categories

**Frontmatter Fields** (from [official docs](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/sub-agents)):

| Field | Required | Description |
|-------|----------|-------------|
| `name` | Yes | Agent identifier (must match filename) |
| `description` | Yes | When to delegate (Claude reads this) |
| `tools` | No | Comma-separated allowed tools |
| `disallowedTools` | No | Comma-separated blocked tools |
| `model` | No | `sonnet`, `opus`, `haiku`, or `inherit` (default) |
| `memory` | No | `user`, `project`, or `local` persistence scope |
| `background` | No | `true` for concurrent execution |
| `maxTurns` | No | Limit API round-trips (cost control) |
| `skills` | No | Comma-separated skills to preload |
| `mcpServers` | No | Comma-separated MCP servers to enable |
| `hooks` | No | Agent-scoped hooks (PreToolUse, PostToolUse, Stop) |
| `isolation` | No | `worktree` for isolated git worktree |
| `permissionMode` | No | Permission level override |

## Available Agents

### Technical Specialists

| Agent | Flag | Description | Primary Use Cases |
|-------|------|-------------|-------------------|
| [architect](agents/architect.md) | `--persona-architect` | Systems architecture specialist | System design, scalability, long-term planning |
| [frontend](agents/frontend.md) | `--persona-frontend` | UX specialist & accessibility advocate | UI components, responsive design, user experience |
| [backend](agents/backend.md) | `--persona-backend` | Reliability engineer & API specialist | Server development, APIs, data integrity |
| [security](agents/security.md) | `--persona-security` | Threat modeler & vulnerability specialist | Security audits, threat analysis, hardening |
| [performance](agents/performance.md) | `--persona-performance` | Optimization & bottleneck specialist | Performance tuning, metrics, optimization |

### Process & Quality Experts

| Agent | Flag | Description | Primary Use Cases |
|-------|------|-------------|-------------------|
| [analyzer](agents/analyzer.md) | `--persona-analyzer` | Root cause & investigation specialist | Debugging, troubleshooting, analysis |
| [qa](agents/qa.md) | `--persona-qa` | Quality advocate & testing specialist | Testing, validation, quality assurance |
| [refactorer](agents/refactorer.md) | `--persona-refactorer` | Code quality & technical debt manager | Code cleanup, refactoring, simplification |
| [devops](agents/devops.md) | `--persona-devops` | Infrastructure & automation specialist | CI/CD, deployment, infrastructure |
| [devops-security-auditor](agents/devops-security-auditor.md) | Special | DevOps security assessment specialist | Infrastructure security, container security |

### Knowledge & Communication

| Agent | Flag | Description | Primary Use Cases |
|-------|------|-------------|-------------------|
| [mentor](agents/mentor.md) | `--persona-mentor` | Educational & knowledge transfer specialist | Teaching, documentation, explanations |
| [scribe](agents/scribe.md) | `--persona-scribe=lang` | Documentation & localization specialist | Professional writing, documentation, translation |

### Project-Specific Agents

| Agent | Model | Extra Features | Primary Use Cases |
|-------|-------|----------------|-------------------|
| [shell-expert](agents/shell-expert.md) | inherit | `skills: fish-reload, dotfiles-sync` | Shell functions, scripts, Fish/Zsh parity |
| [test-runner](agents/test-runner.md) | haiku | `background: true`, `maxTurns: 10` | Running test suites, reporting results |
| [dotfiles-doctor](agents/dotfiles-doctor.md) | haiku | `maxTurns: 15` | Stow validation, tool checks, theme consistency |

### Compact Caveman Agents

Use these when the task has a known shape and the parent context needs operational signal without prose. Do not use them for open-ended architecture, security threat modeling, mentoring, or requirements discovery.

| Agent | Model | Extra Features | Primary Use Cases |
|-------|-------|----------------|-------------------|
| [caveman-investigator](agents/caveman-investigator.md) | haiku | `maxTurns: 8` | Root-cause evidence, file location, compact debug handoff |
| [caveman-reviewer](agents/caveman-reviewer.md) | inherit | `maxTurns: 10` | Findings-only review with severity, file:line, fix |
| [caveman-tester](agents/caveman-tester.md) | haiku | `background: true`, `maxTurns: 8` | Targeted validation receipts |
| [caveman-builder](agents/caveman-builder.md) | inherit | `maxTurns: 12` | Surgical execution of already-defined small changes |
| [caveman-checklist](agents/caveman-checklist.md) | haiku | `maxTurns: 6` | Terse execution checklist for already-scoped work |

## Agent Activation

### Manual Activation
Use flags to explicitly activate agents:
```bash
--persona-architect           # Activate architect agent
--persona-frontend --persona-qa  # Activate multiple agents
--persona-scribe=es          # Activate scribe with Spanish language
```

### Auto-Activation
Agents activate automatically based on:
- **Keywords**: Domain-specific terms trigger relevant agents
- **Context**: Project type and current task determine agent selection
- **Commands**: Specific commands have preferred agents
- **Complexity**: High complexity triggers analytical agents

### Command Integration
Commands work seamlessly with agents:
```bash
/build --persona-frontend     # Frontend-focused build
/analyze                       # Auto-activates analyzer, architect, security
/improve --persona-refactorer # Refactoring-focused improvements
/test --persona-qa            # QA-focused testing
```

## Cross-Agent Collaboration

Agents can work together for complex tasks:

### Common Collaboration Patterns
- **architect + performance**: System design with performance optimization
- **security + backend**: Secure server-side development
- **frontend + qa**: User-focused development with testing
- **mentor + scribe**: Educational content creation
- **analyzer + refactorer**: Root cause analysis with code improvement
- **devops + security**: Infrastructure automation with security

### Conflict Resolution
When multiple agents are active:
1. **Primary Agent**: Leads decision-making within domain
2. **Consulting Agents**: Provide specialized input
3. **Validation Agents**: Review decisions for quality
4. **Priority Matrix**: Resolves conflicts using agent hierarchies

## Flag Integration

All agents work with the complete flag system:

### Thinking Flags
- `--think`: Enhanced analysis (4K tokens)
- `--think-hard`: Deep analysis (10K tokens)
- `--ultrathink`: Maximum analysis (32K tokens)

### MCP Server Flags
- `--seq`: Sequential reasoning
- `--c7`: Context7 documentation
- `--magic`: UI component generation
- `--play`: Playwright testing

### Optimization Flags
- `--uc`: Ultra-compressed output
- `--validate`: Pre-operation validation
- `--safe-mode`: Conservative execution
- `--loop`: Iterative improvement

### Orchestration Flags
- `--wave-mode`: Multi-stage execution
- `--delegate`: Sub-agent delegation
- `--concurrency`: Parallel processing

## Agent Selection Guide

### By Task Type
- **Building**: frontend, backend, architect
- **Debugging**: analyzer, qa, performance
- **Security**: security, devops-security-auditor
- **Documentation**: scribe, mentor
- **Optimization**: performance, refactorer
- **Infrastructure**: devops, architect

### By Project Phase
- **Planning**: architect, analyzer
- **Implementation**: frontend, backend
- **Testing**: qa, performance
- **Deployment**: devops, security
- **Maintenance**: refactorer, analyzer

### By Complexity
- **Simple tasks**: Single specialized agent
- **Moderate tasks**: Primary + consulting agent
- **Complex tasks**: Multiple collaborative agents
- **Critical tasks**: Full agent team with validation

## Configuration

Agents respect all system configurations:
- Token limits and compression settings
- MCP server availability
- Resource management thresholds
- Quality gate requirements
- Validation cycles

## Best Practices

1. **Let auto-activation work**: The system usually selects the right agents
2. **Use explicit flags for precision**: Override when you need specific expertise
3. **Combine agents for complex tasks**: Multiple perspectives improve outcomes
4. **Trust agent expertise**: Each agent excels in their domain
5. **Monitor agent recommendations**: Agents provide domain-specific insights

## Quick Reference

```bash
# Single agent activation
--persona-frontend

# Multiple agents
--persona-architect --persona-security

# Agent with language
--persona-scribe=fr

# Agent with other flags
--persona-performance --think-hard --uc

# Command with agent
/build --persona-backend --validate
```

For detailed information about each agent, click on the agent name in the tables above to view their full specification.

## Agent Teams Integration

The persona-based agent system complements Claude Code's native **Agent Teams** feature (experimental). Agent Teams spawn separate Claude Code instances that communicate via peer-to-peer messaging, while personas operate within a single session.

### When to Use Each
| Scenario | Best Approach |
|----------|--------------|
| Single-session domain expertise | Personas (`--persona-*` flags) |
| Parallel same-repo collaboration | Agent Teams (teammates with shared task list) |
| Isolated multi-branch work | `gwt-parallel` (devcontainer + worktree per branch) |
| Autonomous ticket execution | `gwt-ticket` + OpenCode + nvim |

### Combining Personas with Agent Teams
Teammates spawned via Agent Teams load CLAUDE.md, so persona auto-activation works within each teammate's session. When creating teams, leverage this by assigning domain-specific context:
```
Spawn a teammate focused on security review (will auto-activate security persona)
Spawn a teammate for frontend components (will auto-activate frontend persona)
```

</system-reminder>

<type>file</type>
<content>
1: // OpenCode plugin: push session metadata into tmux environment variables.
2: // tmux status bar segments can read these via #{E:OPENCODE_SESSION_ID}, etc.
3: // Requires tmux to be running — degrades silently if not.
4: import type { Plugin } from "@opencode-ai/plugin"
5: 
6: export const TmuxStatusPlugin: Plugin = async ({ $, directory }) => {
7:   let currentSessionID: string | null = null
8:   let currentModel: string | null = null
9: 
10:   async function setTmuxEnv(key: string, value: string) {
11:     try {
12:       await $`tmux set-environment -g ${key} ${value}`.quiet().nothrow()
13:     } catch {
14:       // tmux not running or not in a tmux session — ignore
15:     }
16:   }
17: 
18:   async function unsetTmuxEnv(key: string) {
19:     try {
20:       await $`tmux set-environment -g -u ${key}`.quiet().nothrow()
21:     } catch {
22:       // ignore
23:     }
24:   }
25: 
26:   function setTmuxEnvSync(key: string, value: string) {
27:     try {
28:       Bun.spawnSync(["tmux", "set-environment", "-g", key, value], {
29:         cwd: directory,
30:         stdout: "ignore",
31:         stderr: "ignore",
32:       })
33:     } catch {
34:       // ignore
35:     }
36:   }
37: 
38:   function unsetTmuxEnvSync(key: string) {
39:     try {
40:       Bun.spawnSync(["tmux", "set-environment", "-g", "-u", key], {
41:         cwd: directory,
42:         stdout: "ignore",
43:         stderr: "ignore",
44:       })
45:     } catch {
46:       // ignore
47:     }
48:   }
49: 
50:   return {
51:     event: async ({ event }) => {
52:       switch (event.type) {
53:         case "session.created": {
54:           const session = (event as any).properties?.info
55:           if (!session?.id) break
56:           currentSessionID = session.id
57:           await setTmuxEnv("OPENCODE_SESSION_ID", session.id)
58:           await setTmuxEnv("OPENCODE_STATUS", "active")
59:           await setTmuxEnv("OPENCODE_DIR", directory)
60:           break
61:         }
62: 
63:         case "message.updated": {
64:           const msg = (event as any).properties?.info
65:           if (msg?.role === "assistant" && msg?.modelID) {
66:             currentModel = msg.modelID
67:             await setTmuxEnv("OPENCODE_MODEL", msg.modelID)
68:           }
69:           break
70:         }
71: 
72:         case "session.status": {
73:           const props = (event as any).properties
74:           const statusType = props?.status?.type
75:           if (statusType) {
76:             setTmuxEnvSync("OPENCODE_STATUS", statusType)
77:           }
78:           break
79:         }
80: 
81:         case "session.deleted":
82:         case "server.instance.disposed": {
83:           currentSessionID = null
84:           currentModel = null
85:           unsetTmuxEnvSync("OPENCODE_SESSION_ID")
86:           unsetTmuxEnvSync("OPENCODE_STATUS")
87:           unsetTmuxEnvSync("OPENCODE_MODEL")
88:           unsetTmuxEnvSync("OPENCODE_DIR")
89:           break
90:         }
91:       }
92:     },
93:   }
94: }

(End of file - total 94 lines)
</content>

<type>file</type>
<content>
1: // OpenCode plugin: capture SSE events for Entire + Diffview integration
2: // Writes a JSONL log for all events and persists diff snapshots so
3: // Diffview (inside Neovim) can replay or inspect AI edits later.
4: // Also mirrors summarized payloads into Entire hooks for checkpoints.
5: import type { Plugin } from "@opencode-ai/plugin"
6: import path from "node:path"
7: import { mkdir, appendFile, writeFile } from "node:fs/promises"
8: import { spawn } from "node:child_process"
9: import crypto from "node:crypto"
10: 
11: type OpencodeEvent = {
12:   type?: string
13:   timestamp?: string
14:   properties?: Record<string, any>
15: }
16: 
17: type NormalizedEvent = {
18:   type: string
19:   timestamp: string
20:   session_id: string | null
21:   message_id: string | null
22:   model: string | null
23:   summary: Record<string, unknown>
24: }
25: 
26: const DIFF_KEYS = [
27:   "patch",
28:   "diff",
29:   "content",
30:   "text",
31:   "body",
32:   "hunks",
33: ]
34: 
35: function looksLikeDiff(candidate: unknown): candidate is string {
36:   if (typeof candidate !== "string") return false
37:   const trimmed = candidate.trimStart()
38:   if (!trimmed) return false
39:   return (
40:     trimmed.startsWith("diff --git") ||
41:     trimmed.startsWith("@@") ||
42:     trimmed.startsWith("--- ") ||
43:     trimmed.includes("\n@@")
44:   )
45: }
46: 
47: function searchForDiff(value: unknown): string | null {
48:   if (!value) return null
49:   if (looksLikeDiff(value)) return value
50:   if (Array.isArray(value)) {
51:     for (const entry of value) {
52:       const diff = searchForDiff(entry)
53:       if (diff) return diff
54:     }
55:     return null
56:   }
57:   if (typeof value === "object") {
58:     for (const key of Object.keys(value as Record<string, unknown>)) {
59:       if (!DIFF_KEYS.includes(key) && typeof (value as any)[key] !== "object") {
60:         continue
61:       }
62:       const diff = searchForDiff((value as any)[key])
63:       if (diff) return diff
64:     }
65:   }
66:   return null
67: }
68: 
69: function sanitizeProperties(props: Record<string, unknown> | undefined) {
70:   if (!props) return {}
71:   const clone = JSON.parse(JSON.stringify(props))
72:   const diff = searchForDiff(clone)
73:   if (diff && diff.length > 2048) {
74:     ;(clone as any).diff_preview = `${diff.slice(0, 1024)}…`
75:   }
76:   return clone
77: }
78: 
79: function extractTarget(props: Record<string, unknown> | undefined): string | null {
80:   if (!props) return null
81:   const candidates = [
82:     props.path,
83:     props.file,
84:     props.filepath,
85:     props.target,
86:     props.targetPath,
87:     props.location,
88:     props?.info?.path,
89:     props?.info?.file,
90:   ]
91:   for (const candidate of candidates) {
92:     if (typeof candidate === "string" && candidate.trim().length > 0) {
93:       return candidate
94:     }
95:   }
96:   return null
97: }
98: 
99: function slugify(value: string | null) {
100:   if (!value) return "workspace"
101:   return value
102:     .toLowerCase()
103:     .replace(/[^a-z0-9]+/g, "-")
104:     .replace(/^-+|-+$/g, "")
105:     .slice(0, 40) || "workspace"
106: }
107: 
108: async function writeJsonl(filePath: string, payload: NormalizedEvent) {
109:   await appendFile(filePath, `${JSON.stringify(payload)}\n`, "utf8")
110: }
111: 
112: function callEntireHook(hook: string, payload: Record<string, unknown>) {
113:   if (process.env.OPENCODE_SSE_DISABLE_ENTIRE === "1") {
114:     return Promise.resolve()
115:   }
116:   return new Promise<void>((resolve) => {
117:     let child: ReturnType<typeof spawn> | null = null
118:     try {
119:       child = spawn("entire", ["hooks", "opencode", hook], {
120:         stdio: ["pipe", "ignore", "ignore"],
121:       })
122:     } catch {
123:       resolve()
124:       return
125:     }
126:     child.on("error", () => resolve())
127:     if (!child.stdin) {
128:       resolve()
129:       return
130:     }
131:     child.stdin.write(`${JSON.stringify(payload)}\n`)
132:     child.stdin.end()
133:     child.on("close", () => resolve())
134:   })
135: }
136: 
137: function normalizeEvent(event: OpencodeEvent): NormalizedEvent {
138:   const props = event.properties ?? {}
139:   const timestamp = event.timestamp ?? new Date().toISOString()
140:   const sessionID =
141:     props.sessionID ??
142:     props.info?.sessionID ??
143:     props.permission?.sessionID ??
144:     props.request?.sessionID ??
145:     null
146:   const model =
147:     props.modelID ??
148:     props.info?.modelID ??
149:     props.meta?.model ??
150:     null
151:   const messageID =
152:     props.messageID ??
153:     props.info?.id ??
154:     props.permission?.messageID ??
155:     null
156: 
157:   return {
158:     type: event.type ?? "unknown",
159:     timestamp,
160:     session_id: sessionID,
161:     message_id: messageID,
162:     model,
163:     summary: sanitizeProperties(props),
164:   }
165: }
166: 
167: async function writeDiffSnapshot(
168:   baseDir: string,
169:   event: NormalizedEvent,
170:   diff: string,
171:   fileHint: string | null,
172: ) {
173:   await mkdir(baseDir, { recursive: true })
174:   const slug = slugify(fileHint)
175:   const stamp = event.timestamp.replace(/[:.]/g, "-")
176:   const id = crypto.randomUUID().slice(0, 8)
177:   const filename = `${stamp}-${slug}-${id}.patch`
178:   const diffPath = path.join(baseDir, filename)
179:   const metaPath = `${diffPath}.json`
180:   await writeFile(diffPath, diff, "utf8")
181:   await writeFile(
182:     metaPath,
183:     JSON.stringify(
184:       {
185:         type: event.type,
186:         timestamp: event.timestamp,
187:         session_id: event.session_id,
188:         message_id: event.message_id,
189:         model: event.model,
190:         target: fileHint,
191:       },
192:       null,
193:       2,
194:     ),
195:     "utf8",
196:   )
197:   await callEntireHook("sse-diff", {
198:     session_id: event.session_id,
199:     message_id: event.message_id,
200:     file: fileHint,
201:     diff_path: diffPath,
202:   })
203: }
204: 
205: export const OpencodeSseRecorderPlugin: Plugin = async ({ directory }) => {
206:   const rootOverride = process.env.OPENCODE_SSE_ROOT
207:   const repoRoot = rootOverride ? path.resolve(rootOverride) : directory
208:   const logDir = path.join(repoRoot, ".entire", "opencode", "sse")
209:   const diffDir = path.join(logDir, "diffs")
210:   const eventLogPath = path.join(logDir, "events.jsonl")
211:   await mkdir(diffDir, { recursive: true })
212: 
213:   return {
214:     event: async ({ event }) => {
215:       if (!event) return
216:       const normalized = normalizeEvent(event as OpencodeEvent)
217:       await writeJsonl(eventLogPath, normalized)
218:       await callEntireHook("sse-event", normalized)
219: 
220:       const diff = searchForDiff(event.properties)

(Showing lines 1-220 of 227. Use offset=221 to continue.)
</content>

<path>/Users/shahe.islam/dotfiles/.config/opencode/plugin/opencode-with-claude.ts</path>
<type>file</type>
<content>
1: import { existsSync } from "node:fs"
2: import type { Plugin } from "@opencode-ai/plugin"
3: 
4: type ClaudePluginModule = {
5:   ClaudeMaxPlugin: Plugin
6: }
7: 
8: let sharedPluginPromise: Promise<Awaited<ReturnType<Plugin>>> | null = null
9: 
10: function seedMeridianProfile(home: string) {
11:   if (process.env.MERIDIAN_PROFILES) {
12:     return
13:   }
14: 
15:   const profilesFile = `${home}/.config/meridian/profiles.json`
16:   if (existsSync(profilesFile)) {
17:     return
18:   }
19: 
20:   process.env.MERIDIAN_PROFILES = JSON.stringify([
21:     {
22:       id: "default",
23:       claudeConfigDir: `${home}/.claude`,
24:     },
25:   ])
26: 
27:   if (!process.env.MERIDIAN_DEFAULT_PROFILE) {
28:     process.env.MERIDIAN_DEFAULT_PROFILE = "default"
29:   }
30: }
31: 
32: async function loadSharedPlugin(input: Parameters<Plugin>[0]) {
33:   if (!sharedPluginPromise) {
34:     sharedPluginPromise = (async () => {
35:       const home = process.env.HOME
36:       if (!home) {
37:         throw new Error("HOME is required to load opencode-with-claude")
38:       }
39: 
40:       seedMeridianProfile(home)
41: 
42:       // OpenCode can initialize plugins multiple times in one process while it
43:       // bootstraps worker/session phases. Reuse a single upstream plugin
44:       // instance so Meridian only starts one Claude proxy per run.
45:       const modulePath = `${home}/.bun/install/global/node_modules/opencode-with-claude/dist/index.js`
46:       const { ClaudeMaxPlugin } = (await import(modulePath)) as ClaudePluginModule
47:       return ClaudeMaxPlugin(input)
48:     })().catch((error) => {
49:       sharedPluginPromise = null
50:       throw error
51:     })
52:   }
53: 
54:   return sharedPluginPromise
55: }
56: 
57: const OpencodeWithClaudePlugin: Plugin = async (input) => {
58:   return loadSharedPlugin(input)
59: }
60: 
61: export default OpencodeWithClaudePlugin

(End of file - total 61 lines)
</content>

parts.text:
**Bottom Line**
Yes, Pi can plausibly reproduce your existing OpenCode setup, including most Claude/OpenCode hook behavior, but not as a drop-in config migration. The hook model is strong enough. The main gaps are OpenCode-specific plugin compatibility, MCP, subagents/task orchestration, permission UX, and your custom account-rotation/SSE/tmux/nvim integrations.

I did read-only inspection only. No files changed.

**Gap Analysis: Pi vs Current OpenCode Setup**

**Source:** `https://github.com/earendil-works/pi`  
**Current setup inspected:** `.config/opencode/`, `.claude/settings.json`, `.opencode/`, related scripts/tests  
**Focus:** Hook parity and OpenCode setup parity  
**Date:** Fri May 15 2026

**Executive Summary**
Pi has enough primitives to recreate the important parts of your OpenCode harness: lifecycle hooks, tool pre/post hooks, context injection, prompt handling, compaction hooks, provider hooks, skills, prompts, commands, custom tools, session state, RPC embedding, and custom UI. The highest-value gaps are not conceptual limitations; they are compatibility gaps because your current setup is encoded as OpenCode plugins and Claude-shaped hook payloads.

**Feature Matrix**

| # | Area | Pi Parity | Notes |
|---|---|---:|---|
| 1 | Tool pre-hooks | Present | Pi `tool_call` can block/mutate before execution. Good match for `use_bun.py`, `validate-bash.py`, `protect-files.py`. |
| 2 | Tool post-hooks | Present | Pi `tool_result` can patch results and trigger side effects. Good match for auto-format, lint-on-save, file-modified, plan-watch. |
| 3 | Prompt submit hooks | Present | Pi `input` and message APIs can reproduce `UserPromptSubmit`, nvim bridge, JFDI prompt context, tmux prompt hooks. |
| 4 | Session start/end hooks | Partial | Pi has session lifecycle events, but current scripts expect Claude/OpenCode-shaped payloads. Needs adapter layer. |
| 5 | Compaction hooks | Present | Pi has `session_before_compact`, `session_compact`, manual/auto compaction, and extension-customizable compaction. |
| 6 | System/context injection | Present | Pi has `before_agent_start`, `context`, system prompt override/append files, skills, and context files. |
| 7 | Provider request/response hooks | Present | Pi has provider hook points and may be stronger than OpenCode here. Minor docs/API naming mismatch observed. |
| 8 | Claude hook reuse | Partial | Achievable by a Pi extension that shells out to existing hook scripts with Claude-compatible JSON payloads. Not native/drop-in. |
| 9 | Skills | Present | Pi supports Agent Skills standard and can load `~/.claude/skills`, `~/.codex/skills`, `.agents/skills`, `.pi/skills`. |
| 10 | Prompt templates / commands | Present | Pi prompt templates and extension commands can cover `.opencode/command/*.md`. |
| 11 | OpenCode agents | Partial | Pi lacks native OpenCode agent files, but can model many through skills/prompts/custom commands. Claude-style subagents are not native. |
| 12 | Subagents / Task tool | Missing in core | Pi explicitly omits subagents. Could be externalized through tmux/worktrees/RPC/extensions, but not built-in. |
| 13 | MCP | Missing in core | Pi explicitly omits MCP. Could be bridged via extensions/tools, but no native MCP parity. |
| 14 | Permission popups | Missing in core | Pi intentionally omits permission popups. Blocking hooks can enforce policy, but UX differs. |
| 15 | Model providers | Present | Pi has broad provider support including Anthropic, OpenAI, Copilot, OpenRouter, Ollama-compatible routes/custom providers. |
| 16 | Claude subscription proxy / Meridian | Partial | Pi supports subscription auth for Claude/OpenAI/Copilot, but current `opencode-with-claude`/Meridian plugin is OpenCode-specific. |
| 17 | OpenAI account rotation | Partial | Pi has provider extensibility, but your replay-on-usage-limit plugin is OpenCode-specific. Needs Pi equivalent. |
| 18 | Cross-provider bridge review | Partial | Pi can run hooks on turn/session idle and inject concerns, but existing bridge wiring is OpenCode-specific. |
| 19 | tmux status integration | Partial | Pi extensions can set tmux env vars, but current `tmux-status.ts` is OpenCode-specific. |
| 20 | nvim open-on-edit | Partial | Pi tool result hooks can reproduce this; current plugin depends on OpenCode tool event shapes. |
| 21 | SSE recorder / Entire diff snapshots | Partial | Pi has event streaming/RPC/session JSONL, but current recorder expects OpenCode events. Needs Pi event normalizer. |
| 22 | Entire integration | Partial | Your OpenCode plugin maps OpenCode lifecycle events to `entire hooks opencode`. Pi can do equivalent via extension. |
| 23 | DCP plugin | Missing as-is | `@tarquinen/opencode-dcp` is OpenCode-specific. Pi would need a DCP-equivalent extension or different mechanism. |
| 24 | PTY plugin | Missing as-is | `opencode-pty` is OpenCode-specific. Pi may cover some terminal behavior natively, but plugin parity is not automatic. |
| 25 | Vibeguard plugin | Missing as-is | `opencode-vibeguard` is OpenCode-specific unless reimplemented as Pi extension/tool hooks. |
| 26 | Session tree/fork/clone | Present | Pi is strong here: JSONL tree sessions, `/tree`, `/fork`, `/clone`, labels/bookmarks. |
| 27 | RPC embedding | Present | Pi RPC over JSONL is useful for replacing some OpenCode/tmux orchestration. |
| 28 | Plan mode | Missing in core | Pi explicitly omits plan mode. Could be emulated with prompts/hooks/permissions, but not native. |
| 29 | Todo tool | Missing in core | Pi intentionally omits built-in todos. Your repo uses Beads anyway, so this is less important. |
| 30 | Hook observability/logging | Partial | Pi exposes rich events, but current logging/reporting scripts need payload adaptation. |

**Top Parity Gaps**

| Rank | Gap | Parity Impact |
|---:|---|---:|
| 1 | Claude/OpenCode hook payload adapter for Pi | 5/5 |
| 2 | MCP replacement/bridge | 5/5 |
| 3 | Subagent/Task orchestration replacement | 5/5 |
| 4 | OpenCode plugin equivalents: DCP, PTY, Vibeguard | 4/5 |
| 5 | OpenAI rotation and prompt replay | 4/5 |
| 6 | SSE recorder / Entire diff integration | 4/5 |
| 7 | tmux/nvim integration extensions | 3/5 |
| 8 | Model routing preset equivalent | 3/5 |
| 9 | Permission UX equivalent | 3/5 |

**Already Covered Well By Pi**

| Current Need | Pi Capability |
|---|---|
| PreToolUse hooks | `tool_call` blocking/mutation |
| PostToolUse hooks | `tool_result` patching/side effects |
| Prompt hooks | `input`, `sendMessage`, `sendUserMessage` |
| Session lifecycle | session events, JSONL sessions |
| Compaction | manual/auto compaction plus extension hooks |
| Context injection | `before_agent_start`, `context`, system prompt files |
| Skills | Agent Skills standard with configurable skill paths |
| Commands | extension commands and prompt templates |
| Provider customization | provider registry, custom providers, provider hooks |
| Session resume/fork/tree | native JSONL tree sessions |
| Embedding/orchestration | JSONL RPC mode |

**Key Caveats**

Pi’s extension model is capable, but your current setup is not portable as-is because it is encoded around OpenCode event names and plugin APIs.

Pi intentionally does not include MCP, subagents, plan mode, permission popups, built-in todos, or background bash. These are the real semantic gaps.

Your existing Claude hook scripts can likely be reused, but only behind an adapter that emits Claude-shaped JSON payloads from Pi events.

The Pi harness docs show some migration/TODO areas around extension facade semantics, lifecycle finalization, tool registry observability, and retry/auto-compaction decision points. I would treat Pi as powerful but less battle-tested for this exact harness shape.

**Answer**
Yes, we can achieve the existing OpenCode setup in Pi for hooks and lifecycle behavior. The likely target architecture would be a Pi extension package that replaces `.config/opencode/plugin/claude-compat.ts`, plus separate Pi extensions for OpenAI rotation, tmux status, nvim open-on-edit, SSE/Entire recording, and any OpenCode-only plugins you still need.

The only things I would not call “native parity” are MCP, subagents/Task, permission popups, and OpenCode plugin compatibility. Those are achievable only through adapters or alternate orchestration, not by Pi configuration alone.
Why are subagents or model routing not configurable through Pi?

They are not **core config concepts** in Pi.

**Subagents**
Pi’s docs explicitly position subagents as out of scope for core. Pi gives you one agent runtime plus extensions, tools, commands, skills, RPC, sessions, and queues. It does not have a native equivalent of Claude/OpenCode subagent files with:

- per-agent prompt/persona
- per-agent tool permissions
- per-agent model
- background execution
- auto-delegation rules
- subagent lifecycle hooks

You could build that as a Pi extension or external tmux/RPC orchestration, but it is not something Pi appears to support declaratively via `.pi/settings.json`.

**Model Routing**
Pi does support model configuration and selection:

- default/current model
- enabled models
- provider registry
- `/model`
- custom providers/models
- model/thinking selection events

What it does **not** appear to have is OpenCode-style **policy routing**, like your `.opencode/model-routing.json` presets:

```json
plan -> claude-opus
execute -> gpt-5.5
batch -> local qwen
triage -> haiku
```

That kind of task-to-model routing depends on command semantics, agent roles, or prompt classification. Pi leaves that to extensions/commands rather than making it a built-in settings feature.

So the precise answer is:

- **Subagents:** not configurable because Pi core intentionally does not implement a subagent abstraction.
- **Model routing:** basic model selection is configurable; automatic task/agent-based routing is not native config, but could be implemented with Pi extensions or command wrappers.

Then tell me how i could set up my opencode so that i have a custom command like /forkworktree that forks but also creates that fork in a new tmux window with a worktree input similar to how we do gwtt <worktree-name>, would that be reasonable?

**Investigating opencode commands**

I need to figure out if opencode custom commands can access arguments. I might have to look through the OpenCode documentation—maybe WebFetch can help? There are local commands to consider too, and I should inspect markdown files like `.opencode/command/skill.md` or `gwt-ticket.fish`. I need to think about how to set things up and whether that would be reasonable. My goal is to understand the architecture without making edits.
I’ll keep this read-only and inspect your existing OpenCode command patterns plus `gwt-ticket`/worktree launch flow so the recommendation matches how your setup actually works.

<skill_content name="customize-opencode">
# Skill: customize-opencode

<!--
  Built-in skill. Name and description are registered in code at
  packages/opencode/src/skill/index.ts (see CUSTOMIZE_OPENCODE_SKILL_NAME
  and CUSTOMIZE_OPENCODE_SKILL_DESCRIPTION). The body below becomes the
  skill's content.
-->

# Customizing opencode

opencode validates its own config strictly and refuses to start when a field
is wrong. The shapes below cover the common surface area, but they are a
**summary, not the source of truth**.

## Full schema reference

The authoritative list of every config option — with field types, enums,
defaults, and descriptions — lives in the published JSON Schema:

**<https://opencode.ai/config.json>**

If a field is not documented in this skill, or you need to confirm an exact
shape before writing config, **fetch that URL and read the schema directly**
rather than guessing. opencode hard-fails on invalid config, so the cost of a
wrong shape is a broken startup.

Independently, every `opencode.json` should declare
`"$schema": "https://opencode.ai/config.json"` so the user's editor catches
mistakes as they type.

## Applying changes

Config is loaded once when opencode starts and is not hot-reloaded. After
saving changes to `opencode.json`, an agent file, a skill, a plugin, or any
other config-time file, **tell the user to quit and restart opencode** for
the changes to take effect. The running session will keep using the
already-loaded config until then.

## Where files live

| Scope                         | Path                                                                                                                      |
| ----------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Project config                | `./opencode.json`, `./opencode.jsonc`, or `.opencode/opencode.json` (opencode walks up from the cwd to the worktree root) |
| Global config                 | `~/.config/opencode/opencode.json` (NOT `~/.opencode/`)                                                                   |
| Project agents                | `.opencode/agent/<name>.md` or `.opencode/agents/<name>.md`                                                               |
| Global agents                 | `~/.config/opencode/agent(s)/<name>.md`                                                                                   |
| Project skills                | `.opencode/skill(s)/<name>/SKILL.md`                                                                                      |
| Global skills                 | `~/.config/opencode/skill(s)/<name>/SKILL.md`                                                                             |
| External skills (auto-loaded) | `~/.claude/skills/<name>/SKILL.md`, `~/.agents/skills/<name>/SKILL.md`                                                    |

Configs from each scope are deep-merged. Project overrides global. Unknown
top-level keys in `opencode.json` are rejected with `ConfigInvalidError`.

## opencode.json

Every field is optional.

```json
{
  "$schema": "https://opencode.ai/config.json",
  "username": "string",
  "model": "provider/model-id",
  "small_model": "provider/model-id",
  "default_agent": "agent-name",
  "shell": "/bin/zsh",
  "logLevel": "DEBUG" | "INFO" | "WARN" | "ERROR",
  "share": "manual" | "auto" | "disabled",
  "autoupdate": true | false | "notify",
  "snapshot": true,
  "instructions": ["AGENTS.md", "docs/style.md"],

  "skills": {
    "paths": [".opencode/skills", "/abs/path/to/skills"],
    "urls": ["https://example.com/.well-known/skills/"]
  },

  "agent": {
    "my-agent": {
      "model": "anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-6",
      "mode": "subagent",
      "description": "...",
      "permission": { "edit": "deny" }
    }
  },

  "command": {
    "deploy": { "description": "...", "prompt": "..." }
  },

  "provider": {
    "anthropic": { "options": { "apiKey": "..." } }
  },
  "disabled_providers": ["openai"],
  "enabled_providers": ["anthropic"],

  "mcp": {
    "playwright": {
      "type": "local",
      "command": ["npx", "-y", "@playwright/mcp"],
      "enabled": true,
      "env": {}
    },
    "remote-thing": {
      "type": "remote",
      "url": "https://...",
      "headers": { "Authorization": "Bearer ..." }
    }
  },

  "plugin": [
    "opencode-gemini-auth",
    "opencode-foo@1.2.3",
    "./local-plugin.ts",
    ["opencode-bar", { "option": "value" }]
  ],

  "permission": {
    "edit": "deny",
    "bash": { "git *": "allow", "*": "ask" }
  },

  "formatter": false,
  "lsp": false,

  "experimental": {
    "primary_tools": ["edit"],
    "mcp_timeout": 30000
  },

  "tool_output": { "max_lines": 200, "max_bytes": 8192 },

  "compaction": { "auto": true, "tail_turns": 15 }
}
```

Shape notes worth being explicit about:

- `model` always carries a provider prefix: `"anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-6"`.
- `skills` is an object with `paths` and/or `urls`, not an array.
- `agent` is an object keyed by agent name, not an array.
- `plugin` is an array of strings or `[name, options]` tuples, not an object.
- `mcp[name].command` is an array of strings, never a single string. `type` is required.
- `permission` is either a string action or an object keyed by tool name.

## Skills

opencode's skill loader scans for `**/SKILL.md` inside skill directories. The
file is named `SKILL.md` exactly, and lives in its own folder named after the
skill:

```
.opencode/skills/my-skill/SKILL.md
```

Frontmatter:

```markdown
---
name: my-skill
description: One sentence covering what this skill does AND when to trigger it. Front-load the literal keywords or filenames the user is likely to say.
---

# My Skill

(skill body in markdown: instructions, examples, references)
```

- `name` is required, lowercase hyphen-separated, up to 64 chars, and matches the folder name.
- `description` is effectively required: skills without one are filtered out and never surfaced to the model. Cover both _what_ the skill does and _when_ to use it. Write in third person ("Use when...", not "I help with..."). Front-load concrete trigger keywords and filenames; gate with "Use ONLY when..." if the skill should stay quiet on adjacent topics.
- Optional: `license`, `compatibility`, `metadata` (string-string map).

Register skills from non-default locations via `skills.paths` (scanned
recursively for `**/SKILL.md`) and `skills.urls` (each URL serves a list of
skills).

## Agents

Two ways to define an agent. Use the file form for anything non-trivial.

### Inline (in `opencode.json`)

```json
{
  "agent": {
    "my-reviewer": {
      "description": "Reviews PRs for style violations.",
      "mode": "subagent",
      "model": "anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-6",
      "permission": { "edit": "deny", "bash": "ask" },
      "prompt": "You are a strict PR reviewer..."
    }
  }
}
```

### File

```
.opencode/agent/my-reviewer.md      OR     .opencode/agents/my-reviewer.md
```

```markdown
---
description: Reviews PRs for style violations.
mode: subagent
model: anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-6
permission:
  edit: deny
  bash: ask
---

You are a strict PR reviewer. Focus on...
```

The file body becomes the agent's `prompt`. Do not also put `prompt:` in the
frontmatter.

`mode` is one of `"primary"`, `"subagent"`, `"all"`.

Allowed top-level frontmatter fields: `name, model, variant, description, mode,
hidden, color, steps, options, permission, disable, temperature, top_p`. Any
unknown field is silently routed into `options`.

To disable a built-in agent: `agent: { build: { disable: true } }`, or in a
file, `disable: true` in frontmatter.

`default_agent` must point to a non-hidden, primary-mode agent.

### Built-in agents

opencode ships with `build`, `plan`, `general`, `explore`, plus optionally
`scout` (gated on `OPENCODE_EXPERIMENTAL_SCOUT`). Hidden internal agents:
`compaction`, `title`, `summary`. To override a built-in's fields, define the
same key in `agent: { <name>: { ... } }`.

## Plugins

`plugin:` is an array. Each entry is one of:

```json
"plugin": [
  "opencode-gemini-auth",            // npm spec, latest
  "opencode-foo@1.2.3",              // npm spec, pinned
  "./local-plugin.ts",               // file path, relative to the declaring config
  "file:///abs/path/plugin.js",      // file URL
  ["opencode-bar", { "key": "val" }] // tuple form with options
]
```

Auto-discovered plugins (no config entry needed): any `*.ts` or `*.js` file in
`.opencode/plugin/` or `.opencode/plugins/`.

A plugin module exports `default` (or any named export) of type
`Plugin = (input: PluginInput, options?) => Promise<Hooks>`. The export is a
function, not a plain object literal, and the function returns an object
(return `{}` if there is nothing to register).

```ts
import type { Plugin } from "@opencode-ai/plugin"

export default (async ({ client, project, directory, $ }) => {
  return {
    config: (cfg) => {
      // cfg is the live merged config; mutate fields here.
    },
    "tool.execute.before": async (input, output) => {
      // mutate output.args before the tool runs
    },
  }
}) satisfies Plugin
```

Hook surface (mutate `output` in place; return `void`):

- `event(input)`: every bus event
- `config(cfg)`: once on init with the merged config
- `chat.message`, `chat.params`, `chat.headers`
- `tool.execute.before`, `tool.execute.after`
- `tool.definition`
- `command.execute.before`
- `shell.env`
- `permission.ask`
- `experimental.chat.messages.transform`, `experimental.chat.system.transform`,
  `experimental.session.compacting`, `experimental.compaction.autocontinue`,
  `experimental.text.complete`

Special object-shaped (not callbacks): `tool: { my_tool: { ... } }`,
`auth: { ... }`, `provider: { ... }`.

## MCP servers

`mcp:` is an object keyed by server name. Each server is discriminated by
`type`:

```json
{
  "mcp": {
    "playwright": {
      "type": "local",
      "command": ["npx", "-y", "@playwright/mcp"],
      "enabled": true,
      "env": { "BROWSER": "chromium" }
    },
    "github": {
      "type": "remote",
      "url": "https://...",
      "enabled": true,
      "headers": { "Authorization": "Bearer ${GITHUB_TOKEN}" }
    },
    "old-server": { "enabled": false }
  }
}
```

`command` is an array of strings. `type` is required. Use `enabled: false` to
disable a server inherited from a parent config.

## Permissions

```json
"permission": {
  "edit": "deny",
  "bash": { "git *": "allow", "rm *": "deny", "*": "ask" },
  "external_directory": { "~/secrets/**": "deny", "*": "allow" }
}
```

Actions: `"allow"`, `"ask"`, `"deny"`.

Per-tool value forms: `"allow"` shorthand (treated as `{"*": "allow"}`), or an
object `{ pattern: action }`. Within an object, **insertion order matters**.
opencode evaluates the LAST matching rule, so put broad rules first and narrow
rules last.

`permission: "allow"` (a string at the top level) is shorthand for "allow
everything" and is rarely what the user wants.

Known permission keys: `read, edit, glob, grep, list, bash, task,
external_directory, todowrite, question, webfetch, websearch, codesearch,
repo_clone, repo_overview, lsp, doom_loop, skill`. Some of these (`todowrite,
question, webfetch, websearch, codesearch, doom_loop`) only accept a flat
action, not a per-pattern object.

`external_directory` patterns are filesystem paths (use `~/`, absolute paths,
or globs like `~/projects/**`).

Per-agent `permission:` overrides top-level `permission:`. Plan Mode lives on
the `plan` agent's permission ruleset (`edit: deny *`).

## Escape hatches

When a user's config is broken and opencode won't start, these env vars help:

- `OPENCODE_DISABLE_PROJECT_CONFIG=1`: skip the project's local `opencode.json`
  and start from globals only. Run from the project directory, opencode loads,
  the user edits the broken file, then they restart without the flag.
- `OPENCODE_CONFIG=/path/to/file.json`: load an additional explicit config.
- `OPENCODE_CONFIG_CONTENT='{"$schema":"https://opencode.ai/config.json"}'`:
  inject inline JSON as a final local-scope merge.
- `OPENCODE_DISABLE_DEFAULT_PLUGINS=1`: skip default plugins.
- `OPENCODE_PURE=1`: skip external plugins entirely.
- `OPENCODE_DISABLE_EXTERNAL_SKILLS=1`,
  `OPENCODE_DISABLE_CLAUDE_CODE_SKILLS=1`: skip the external skill scans under
  `~/.claude/` and `~/.agents/`.

## When proposing edits

- Validate against the schema before writing. If you are unsure of a field's
  exact shape, or the field is not covered in this skill, fetch
  `https://opencode.ai/config.json` and read the schema rather than guessing.
- Preserve `$schema` and any existing fields the user did not ask to change.
- For agent, skill, and plugin definitions, prefer creating new files in the
  correct location over inlining everything in `opencode.json`.
- If the user's existing config is malformed, point them at the env-var escape
  hatches above so they can edit from inside opencode without breaking their
  session.
- After saving any config change, remind the user to quit and restart opencode
  — running sessions keep using the already-loaded config.

23:     #   --local         Use local Ollama model (default: qwen3-coder)

1417:             gwt-dev $gwt_args </dev/null >/dev/null 2>&1
1418:         else
1419:             gwt-dev $gwt_args </dev/null
1420:         end
1421:         if test $status -ne 0
1422:             echo "Error: Failed to create worktree"
1423:             return 1
1424:         end
1425:     else
1426:         if not $quiet_mode
1427:             echo "Worktree already exists, reusing..."
1428:         end
1429:     end
1430: 
1431:     # Resolve worktree path
1432:     set worktree_path (realpath $worktree_path)
1433: 
1434:     # Harness init: activate pre-commit hooks and validate environment in worktree
1435:     set -l harness_init "$worktree_path/scripts/harness/init.sh"

1448:     if $quiet_mode

1453:     # Background metadata operations (bd + agent-cv) — tracking only, not needed

1455:     # Saves ~4.5s (bd init 2.2s + bd create 1.5s + hook bead 0.75s).
1456:     begin
1457:         # Beads agent memory: init → work bead → hook bead → swarm
1458:         if command -q bd

1461:                 bd init --quiet >/dev/null 2>&1; or true
1462:             end
1463:             if test -d "$worktree_path/.beads"
1464:                 cd $worktree_path
1465:                 set -l bd_create_args "$title" --external-ref "$issue_key" --description "$description" --silent
1466:                 if test -n "$bead_priority"
1467:                     set -a bd_create_args --priority $bead_priority
1468:                 end
1469:                 bd create $bd_create_args >/dev/null 2>&1; or true
1470:                 # GUPP Hook bead: ephemeral work-slung marker per Gastown Universal Propulsion Principle
1471:                 bd create "hook: $issue_key" \
1472:                     --ephemeral \
1473:                     --type event \
1474:                     --event-category "agent.hooked" \
1475:                     --event-target "$issue_key" \
1476:                     --labels "gt:hook,gt:gupp" \
1477:                     --silent >/dev/null 2>&1; or true
1478:                 # Agent bead: enables bd agent state/heartbeat tracking
1479:                 set -l agent_name "witness-"(basename $worktree_path)
1480:                 set -l agent_bead_id (bd q "$agent_name" --type task --labels "gt:agent" 2>/dev/null)

1483:                     bd kv set "agent.bead_id" "$agent_bead_id" >/dev/null 2>&1; or true
1484:                 end
1485:                 # Swarm molecule from epic (conditional)
1486:                 if test -n "$swarm_epic_id"

1488:                 end
1489:             end
1490:         end
1491:         # Agent CV
1492:         set -l cv_script "$HOME/dotfiles/scripts/agent-cv.sh"

1495:             if test -n "$sub_profile"

1497:             end
1498:             if test -n "$local_model"

1500:             end

1502:         end
1503:     end </dev/null &
1504:     disown 2>/dev/null
1505:

1694:         else

1697: 
1698: $description
1699: 
1700: Instructions:

1710:         end

1714:     if $use_dynamic_beads; and command -q bd
1715:         set -l beads_suffix "
1716: 
1717: BEADS SUBTASK TRACKING — A parent bead was created for this ticket (external-ref: $issue_key).
1718: 
1719: ALWAYS decompose multi-step tasks into subtasks. Only skip decomposition for true single-file, single-change fixes.
1720: 
1721: Workflow:
1722: 1. Find the parent bead: bd list --status=open
1723: 2. Create subtasks linked to parent:
1724:    bd create --title='SUBTASK_TITLE' --description='WHY_AND_WHAT' --type=task --priority=2 --parent PARENT_BEAD_ID
1725:    Use 'bd dep add CHILD_ID BLOCKER_ID' if ordering matters.
1726: 3. Work each subtask:
1727:    bd update BEAD_ID --status=in_progress → code → run tests if applicable → commit → bd close BEAD_ID
1728:    Hooks enforce syntax and formatting on commit. Focus on testing and bead lifecycle.
1729: 4. Between subtasks: bd ready (shows what is unblocked next)

1735: - ALWAYS use 'bd q \"discovered issue\"' to capture issues found during work (silent, returns only ID)
1736: - ALWAYS use 'bd update ID --append-notes \"finding or decision\"' to record findings
1737: - ALWAYS use 'bd search \"keyword\"' to check for related beads before creating new ones
1738: - Close multiple: bd close ID1 ID2 ID3 (batch close is more efficient)
1739: - Check blocked: bd blocked (see what needs unblocking)
1740: - Store metadata: bd kv set key value (persistent across compactions)

1778: The plan is your persistent memory. Hooks will re-inject it after compaction."

1794: 5. Only after the plan is complete, switch to execution and implement

1796:         if test -n "$prompt_suffix"
