1: function gwt-dev --description "Create worktree with isolated devcontainer"
2:     # Usage: gwt-dev <branch> [--exec] [--new] [--features python,node] [--mount <dir>] [--no-devcon]
3:     #
4:     # Creates a git worktree in ../repo-branch format and optionally launches
5:     # a devcontainer with isolated storage using the worktree name as instance.
6:     #
7:     # Options:
8:     #   --exec, -e      Enter container shell after starting
9:     #   --new, -n       Create new branch (like gwtabf instead of gwtaf)
10:     #   --features, -F  Add devcontainer features (comma-separated or multiple -F)
11:     #   --mount, -m     Add additional directory mount (repeatable)
12:     #   --no-devcon     Create worktree only, skip devcontainer
13:     #   --rebuild, -r   Rebuild devcontainer (remove existing)
14:     #   --fast, -f      Skip lifecycle hooks in devcontainer
15:     #   --no-cd         Don't cd into the worktree (useful when called from other functions)
16:     #   --help, -h      Show this help
17: 
18:     # Parse arguments
19:     set -l branch ""
20:     set -l do_exec false
21:     set -l do_new false
22:     set -l do_no_devcon false
23:     set -l do_no_cd false
24:     set -l do_rebuild false
25:     set -l do_fast false
26:     set -l show_help false
27:     set -l features
28:     set -l mounts
29:     set -l skip_next false
30: 
31:     for i in (seq (count $argv))
32:         if $skip_next
33:             set skip_next false
34:             continue
35:         end
36: 
37:         set -l arg $argv[$i]
38: 
39:         switch $arg
40:             case --exec -e
41:                 set do_exec true
42:             case --new -n
43:                 set do_new true
44:             case --no-devcon
45:                 set do_no_devcon true
46:             case --no-cd
47:                 set do_no_cd true
48:             case --rebuild -r
49:                 set do_rebuild true
50:             case --fast -f
51:                 set do_fast true
52:             case --help -h
53:                 set show_help true
54:             case --features -F
55:                 # Next arg is features (comma-separated)
56:                 set -l next_i (math $i + 1)
57:                 if test $next_i -le (count $argv)
58:                     # Split by comma and add each feature
59:                     for feature in (string split "," $argv[$next_i])
60:                         set -a features $feature
61:                     end
62:                     set skip_next true
63:                 else
64:                     echo "Error: --features requires a value"
65:                     return 1
66:                 end
67:             case --mount -m
68:                 # Next arg is directory to mount
69:                 set -l next_i (math $i + 1)
70:                 if test $next_i -le (count $argv)
71:                     set -l mount_path $argv[$next_i]
72:                     # Expand path and validate
73:                     set -l expanded_path (string replace -r '^\~' "$HOME" $mount_path)
74:                     if test -d "$expanded_path"
75:                         set -a mounts (realpath $expanded_path)
76:                     else
77:                         echo "Error: Mount directory not found: $mount_path"
78:                         return 1
79:                     end
80:                     set skip_next true
81:                 else
82:                     echo "Error: --mount requires a directory path"
83:                     return 1
84:                 end
85:             case '-*'
86:                 echo "Error: Unknown option: $arg"
87:                 return 1
88:             case '*'
89:                 if test -z "$branch"
90:                     set branch $arg
91:                 else
92:                     echo "Error: Multiple branches specified"
93:                     return 1
94:                 end
95:         end
96:     end
97: 
98:     # Show help
99:     if $show_help
100:         echo "Usage: gwt-dev <branch> [options]"
101:         echo ""
102:         echo "Create a git worktree with isolated devcontainer environment."
103:         echo ""
104:         echo "Options:"
105:         echo "  --exec, -e      Enter container shell after starting"
106:         echo "  --new, -n       Create new branch (instead of checking out existing)"
107:         echo "  --features, -F  Add devcontainer features (comma-separated)"
108:         echo "  --mount, -m     Add additional directory mount (repeatable)"
109:         echo "  --no-devcon     Create worktree only, skip devcontainer"
110:         echo "  --no-cd         Don't cd into the worktree after creation"
111:         echo "  --rebuild, -r   Rebuild devcontainer (remove existing container)"
112:         echo "  --fast, -f      Skip devcontainer lifecycle hooks"
113:         echo "  --help, -h      Show this help"
114:         echo ""
115:         echo "Feature shortcuts: python, node, go, rust, java, ruby, php, dotnet,"
116:         echo "                   aws, azure, gcloud, terraform, kubectl, docker"
117:         echo ""
118:         echo "Examples:"
119:         echo "  gwt-dev feature/auth               # Worktree + detect devcontainer"
120:         echo "  gwt-dev feature/auth --exec        # + enter container"
121:         echo "  gwt-dev feature/new --new --exec   # Create new branch + enter"
122:         echo "  gwt-dev hotfix/123 --no-devcon     # Worktree only"
123:         echo "  gwt-dev feat -F python,node -e     # With extra features"
124:         echo "  gwt-dev feat -m ../repo-main -e    # Mount another worktree for context"
125:         echo "  gwt-dev feat -m ~/ref -m ~/docs    # Multiple additional mounts"
126:         echo ""
127:         echo "The worktree is created at ../repo-branch and the devcontainer"
128:         echo "instance uses the same name for volume isolation."
129:         echo ""
130:         echo "Additional mounts are available at /mounts/<dirname> inside the container."
131:         return 0
132:     end
133: 
134:     # Validate branch argument
135:     if test -z "$branch"
136:         echo "Error: Branch name required"
137:         echo "Usage: gwt-dev <branch> [--exec] [--new] [--features ...] [--no-devcon]"
138:         return 1
139:     end
140: 
141:     # Get repository name and construct worktree path (resolve to main repo, not worktree)
142:     # Also serves as git-repo validation (replaces removed guard at top)
143:     set -l git_common_dir (git rev-parse --git-common-dir 2>/dev/null)
144:     or begin
145:         echo "Error: Not in a git repository"
146:         return 1
147:     end
148:     set -l repo_root (realpath "$git_common_dir/..")
149:     set -l repo (basename $repo_root)
150:     set -l worktree_name "$repo-$branch"
151:     set -l worktree_path "$repo_root/../$worktree_name"
152: 
153:     # Clean up branch name for instance naming (replace / with -)
154:     set -l instance_name (string replace -a "/" "-" $worktree_name)
155: 
156:     # Check if worktree already exists
157:     if test -d "$worktree_path"
158:         echo "Worktree already exists: $worktree_path"
159:         echo "Switching to existing worktree..."
160:     else
161:         # Create worktree
162:         echo "Creating worktree: $worktree_path"
163:         if $do_new
164:             # Create new branch + worktree
165:             if not git worktree add -b $branch $worktree_path
166:                 echo "Error: Failed to create worktree with new branch"
167:                 return 1
168:             end
169:         else
170:             # Add worktree for existing branch
171:             if not git worktree add $worktree_path $branch
172:                 echo "Error: Failed to create worktree"
173:                 echo "Tip: Use --new (-n) to create a new branch"
174:                 return 1
175:             end
176:         end
177:     end
178: 
179:     # Cache realpath once for reuse below
180:     set -l abs_wt (realpath $worktree_path)
181: 
182:     # Trust mise config if present — must run before any early return
183:     # so worktrees created with --no-devcon (e.g. gwt-ticket) are also trusted
184:     if command -q mise
185:         if test -f "$abs_wt/mise.toml"; or test -f "$abs_wt/.mise.toml"
186:             mise trust "$abs_wt" 2>/dev/null
187:             echo "   mise trusted: $abs_wt"
188:         end
189:     end
190: 
191:     # Skip devcontainer if requested
192:     if $do_no_devcon
193:         echo "Worktree created: $worktree_path"
194:         if not $do_no_cd
195:             cd $abs_wt
196:             echo "   Switched to: "(pwd)
197:         end
198:         return 0
199:     end
200: 
201:     # Always use the built-in devcon claude sandbox for isolation.
202:     # The devcon function uses ~/dotfiles/devcontainer/claude-code-plugins/
203:     # so the project does NOT need its own .devcontainer/ directory.
204:     echo "Launching devcontainer sandbox with instance: $instance_name"
205: 
206:     # Run structured setup (port allocation, agent sync, MCP sync, beads init)
207:     gwt-setup $worktree_path
208: 
209:     # Build devcon arguments
210:     set -l devcon_args claude -i $instance_name
211: 
212:     # Add features
213:     for feature in $features
214:         set -a devcon_args -F $feature
215:     end
216: 
217:     # Add flags
218:     if $do_rebuild
219:         set -a devcon_args --rebuild
220:     end
221:     if $do_fast
222:         set -a devcon_args --fast
223:     end
224:     if $do_exec
225:         set -a devcon_args --exec
226:     end
227: 
228:     # Mount the worktree as additional mount (reuse cached realpath)
229:     set -a devcon_args $abs_wt
230: 
231:     # Add additional mounts
232:     for mount in $mounts
233:         set -a devcon_args $mount
234:     end
235: 
236:     # Launch devcontainer
237:     echo "Running: devcon $devcon_args"
238:     devcon $devcon_args
239: 
240:     echo ""
241:     echo "Worktree + Devcontainer ready:"
242:     echo "   Worktree: $worktree_path"
243:     echo "   Instance: $instance_name"
244:     echo "   Mount:    /mounts/$worktree_name"
245:     if test (count $mounts) -gt 0
246:         echo "   Additional mounts:"
247:         for mount in $mounts
248:             echo "             /mounts/"(basename $mount)
249:         end
250:     end
251: end

1: #!/usr/bin/env bash
2: # Launch OpenCode inside tmux with alternate-screen disabled so tmux copy-mode
3: # retains scrollback. Restores the prior setting when OpenCode exits.
4: set -euo pipefail
5: 
6: # Determine current window id so we can scope setw calls via tmux target
7: WINDOW=${TMUX_PANE:-}
8: 
9: restore_alternate_screen() {
10: 	if [ -n "$WINDOW" ]; then
11: 		tmux setw -t "$WINDOW" alternate-screen on >/dev/null 2>&1 || true
12: 	else
13: 		tmux setw -w alternate-screen on >/dev/null 2>&1 || true
14: 	fi
15: }
16: 
17: trap restore_alternate_screen EXIT
18: 
19: if [ -n "$WINDOW" ]; then
20: 	tmux setw -t "$WINDOW" alternate-screen off >/dev/null 2>&1 || true
21: else
22: 	tmux setw -w alternate-screen off >/dev/null 2>&1 || true
23: fi
24: 
25: exec "$HOME/dotfiles/scripts/bin/opencode"

#!/usr/bin/env bash
# Launch OpenCode inside tmux with alternate-screen disabled so tmux copy-mode
# retains scrollback. Restores the prior setting when OpenCode exits.
set -euo pipefail

# Determine current window id so we can scope setw calls via tmux target
WINDOW=${TMUX_PANE:-}

restore_alternate_screen() {
	if [ -n "$WINDOW" ]; then
		tmux setw -t "$WINDOW" alternate-screen on >/dev/null 2>&1 || true
	else
		tmux setw -w alternate-screen on >/dev/null 2>&1 || true
	fi
}

trap restore_alternate_screen EXIT

if [ -n "$WINDOW" ]; then
	tmux setw -t "$WINDOW" alternate-screen off >/dev/null 2>&1 || true

parts.text:
**Considering command execution**

I don't need to provide exact final code for the user's request on how to do something. It's important to mention that `!` commands in OpenCode custom commands are executed during command expansion, affecting the context. I might propose using a wrapper in Fish since conventions dictate that Bash scripts use Bash and Fish functions are placed in a specific location. I aim to ensure the command is callable from an OpenCode non-interactive shell, which likely uses Fish. Overall, flexibility is key in scripting.

**Clarifying function and script structure**

I'm noticing that Fish functions should go in `.config/fish/functions`, while the Shell Scripts section mentions a preference for Bash conventions. For my plan, I want to propose a Fish function called `opencode-forkworktree.fish`, maybe with a small Bash wrapper. If I commit to a standalone script within `scripts/opencode`, I'll conform to the existing Bash conventions. It seems better to create this Fish function directly, allowing for command calls that route through Fish. Ultimately, the goal is to maintain clarity and efficiency.

**Establishing a base**

I need to use the current HEAD as the base for whatever I'm working on. This seems essential for ensuring that the operations I'm planning are grounded in the latest state of the project. I'll make sure to reference this correctly as I proceed, since having a solid base will help maintain the integrity of further actions. Let's ensure I stay on track with the most up-to-date information.

**Considering limitations in Markdown**

I should mention that the Markdown command can’t literally create an interactive prompt asking for a "worktree name"; instead, I need to use `$ARGUMENTS` as the input. If I want to have a prompt for UI selection or confirmation, I might consider implementing an OpenCode plugin with `command.execute.before` and `client.tui`. The existing plugin utilizes `client.tui.showToast`, so perhaps I can prompt in a different way? I need to be careful about overclaiming and focus on the arguments instead.

Yes, reasonable. I’d set it up as a thin OpenCode slash command that delegates to your existing `gwt-ticket`/`gwt-dev` machinery rather than rebuilding tmux/worktree logic inside OpenCode.

**Recommended Shape**
Create `/forkworktree` as:

- `.opencode/command/forkworktree.md`
- A small Fish function or script, e.g. `opencode-forkworktree`
- Internally call `gwt-ticket <worktree-name> <prompt> --opencode --no-edit`

That reuses the pieces you already trust:

- `gwt-dev` creates `../repo-branch`
- `gwt-ticket` creates the tmux window
- OpenCode launches in the left pane
- nvim and terminal panes are created
- hooks, Beads, Entire, plan/changelog, bridge env, OpenCode model handling all stay consistent

**Command UX**
Something like:

```text
/forkworktree my-spike investigate whether the auth refactor works better here
```

Would create:

```text
branch: my-spike
worktree: ../repo-my-spike
tmux: <repo>:my-spike
OpenCode prompt: investigate whether the auth refactor works better here
```

**Important Limitation**
A plain `.opencode/command/*.md` command can use `$ARGUMENTS` and run shell blocks, but it is not a true “fork this OpenCode conversation with full transcript” primitive.

So there are two levels:

| Goal | Best Implementation |
|---|---|
| Fork current git state into a new worktree and start OpenCode there | OpenCode command + Fish wrapper calling `gwt-ticket` |
| Also carry current conversation transcript/session state into the new OpenCode session | Add an OpenCode plugin that tracks current session/messages, then passes a summary/transcript to the wrapper |

**Minimal Plan**
1. Add `.opencode/command/forkworktree.md`.
2. Add `opencode-forkworktree.fish` in `.config/fish/functions/`.
3. Parse first arg as worktree/branch name; rest as the seed prompt.
4. Call:

```fish
gwt-ticket "$worktree_name" "$prompt" --opencode --no-edit
```

5. Optionally pass current model:

```fish
--opencode-model "$OPENCODE_PRIMARY_MODEL"
```

6. Add tests to `scripts/test-filter.sh opencode` for the new command/function.

**Better Version**
Make `/forkworktree` accept:

```text
/forkworktree <name> [prompt]
```

If prompt is omitted, seed it with something like:

```text
Continue from the current branch in a fresh worktree. First inspect the current diff and branch context, then proceed independently.
```

**Would I Do It?**
Yes. I’d start with the simple command-wrapper version. It gives you 80% of the value with almost no new architecture. Only add a plugin-backed “true conversation fork” if you actually need the new OpenCode instance to inherit the current chat transcript.
Doesn't gwtt already default to opencode?

Yes. `gwt-ticket`/`gwtt` already defaults to OpenCode.

The wrapper would not need `--opencode`; I included it only as an explicit reminder. The actual call can just be:

```fish
gwtt "$worktree_name" "$prompt" --no-edit
```

Or with model override only when needed:

```fish
gwtt "$worktree_name" "$prompt" --no-edit --opencode-model openai/gpt-5.5
```

So `/forkworktree` can be very thin:

```text
/forkworktree my-spike investigate auth refactor
```

maps to:

```fish
gwtt my-spike "investigate auth refactor" --no-edit
```

That will create the worktree, tmux window, OpenCode pane, nvim pane, and terminal pane using the existing default path.
So basically I can use / commands in opencode to trigger literaly commands and this can be the hook that we use?

Yes, basically.

OpenCode `/` commands can be used as **user-triggered automation entrypoints**. In your setup, a command like `/forkworktree` can call a real shell command, and that shell command can call `gwtt`.

Important distinction:

- A `/` command is not a lifecycle hook like `tool.execute.before`.
- It is a manual trigger that expands a command prompt and can run shell snippets.
- If the command calls `gwtt`, that becomes the “hook” into your existing tmux/worktree/OpenCode orchestration.

Best pattern:

```text
/forkworktree my-spike investigate auth refactor
```

OpenCode command delegates to:

```fish
opencode-forkworktree my-spike investigate auth refactor
```

Fish wrapper delegates to:

```fish
gwtt my-spike "investigate auth refactor" --no-edit
```

That is reasonable because `gwtt` already owns:

- worktree creation
- tmux window creation
- OpenCode launch
- nvim/terminal pane layout
- hooks/Beads/Entire/plan/changelog setup

I would not put complex parsing directly in the `.opencode/command/*.md` file. Use the slash command as a thin trigger, then keep the real behavior in a Fish function or script.
Make this change so that we can test it out

**Implementing multi-step tasks**

I need to respond by implementing some changes. I might consider using a todo list for organization, since this is a multi-step task: creating a command, a Fish function, and potentially updating the setup. We already have the necessary context, so following the instructions to use apply_patch for edits is important. I need to look closely at implementing the Fish function and how shell blocks behave, especially regarding slash commands. There are a lot of details to think about here!

**Designing shell commands**

I want to ensure that shell blocks work correctly when triggering side effects. For launching a new OpenCode worktree, I need a command structure that properly handles arguments. The challenge here is managing `$ARGUMENTS` safely due to special characters and spaces during shell expansion. It seems like a good idea to use a command file for clarity. I should clearly describe the command and report its output without making additional changes. I need to think about making the command more robust by writing `$ARGUMENTS` as needed.

**Inspecting command behavior**

I need to figure out how the `!` command in markdown interacts with shell commands after template expansion. It looks like I can’t access raw arguments safely unless OpenCode supports using environment variables. I might have to inspect the relevant documentation or schema to clarify how this all works. It’s important to understand these interactions before proceeding to ensure everything functions correctly! So I’ll take some time to delve into the docs.
I’ll add this as a thin OpenCode command plus a Fish wrapper that delegates to `gwtt`, then run the targeted Fish/OpenCode checks.

# Phase 6: Documentation
    run_test "CLAUDE.md documents ckpt-agent flag" "grep -q 'ckpt-agent' '$DOTFILES_ROOT/CLAUDE.md'"
    run_test "CLAUDE.md documents attribution subcommand" "grep -q 'attribution' '$DOTFILES_ROOT/CLAUDE.md'"
}

test_opencode() {
    echo -e "${BLUE}--- OpenCode Tests ---${NC}"

    run_test "OpenCode config exists" "[ -f '$DOTFILES_ROOT/.config/opencode/opencode.json' ]"
    run_test "OpenCode config is valid JSON" "jq empty '$DOTFILES_ROOT/.config/opencode/opencode.json'"
    run_test "OpenCode permissions are blanket allow" "[ \"\$(jq -r '.permission' '$DOTFILES_ROOT/.config/opencode/opencode.json')\" = 'allow' ]"
    run_test "OpenCode config has openai provider" "jq -e '.provider.openai' '$DOTFILES_ROOT/.config/opencode/opencode.json' >/dev/null 2>&1"
    run_test "OpenCode config has gpt-5.4 model" "jq -e '.provider.openai.models[\"gpt-5.4\"]' '$DOTFILES_ROOT/.config/opencode/opencode.json' >/dev/null 2>&1"
    run_test "OpenCode Vim tap configured" "grep -q 'tap \"leohenon/tap\"' '$DOTFILES_ROOT/homebrew/Brewfile'"
    run_test "OpenCode Vim formula configured" "grep -q 'brew \"leohenon/tap/ocv\"' '$DOTFILES_ROOT/homebrew/Brewfile'"
    run_test "Legacy anomalyco OpenCode formula removed" "! grep -q 'anomalyco/tap/opencode' '$DOTFILES_ROOT/homebrew/Brewfile'"
    run_test "OpenCode compatibility shim exists" "[ -f '$DOTFILES_ROOT/scripts/bin/opencode' ]"
    run_test "OpenCode compatibility shim executable" "[ -x '$DOTFILES_ROOT/scripts/bin/opencode' ]"
    run_test "OpenCode compatibility shim delegates to ocv" "grep -q 'exec ocv \"\$@\"' '$DOTFILES_ROOT/scripts/bin/opencode'"
    run_test "OpenCode tmux launcher uses shim" "grep -q 'exec \"\$HOME/dotfiles/scripts/bin/opencode\"' '$DOTFILES_ROOT/scripts/opencode/tmux-open.sh'"

function gwtt --description "Launch gwt-ticket without holding the caller pane"
    set -l run_foreground false
    set -l desc_file_next false
    set -l passthrough_args

    if test (count $argv) -eq 0
        set run_foreground true
    end

    for arg in $argv
        if $desc_file_next
            if test "$arg" = "-"
                set run_foreground true
            end
            set desc_file_next false
            set -a passthrough_args "$arg"
            continue
        end

        switch $arg

state.title: .config/fish/functions/gwtt.fish

parts.text:
**Considering function creation**

I’m thinking about creating a Fish function that either calls `gwt-ticket` or `gwtt`. When called from within OpenCode, I should use `gwtt` to avoid holding the pane since it backgrounds by default when provided arguments. It's important to ensure that `gwt-ticket` is loaded automatically if it's in the Fish function path. I also need to parse the arguments effectively, considering the risks with command execution and ensuring user-triggered automation.

**Examining command execution**

I'm looking into how to support the `!` shell in command files. If I place this: 

```
!`fish -lc 'opencode-forkworktree $argv' -- $ARGUMENTS`
```

I need to ensure it executes robustly. The content in backticks gets run by the opencode shell after replacing args, but if `$ARGUMENTS` contains spaces, it might split into multiple words. That's fine, as long as the first token is the name and the rest are prompt words. However, if prompts include quotes or special characters, it could fail. We want to keep the command instructions simple and practical.

**Clarifying Fish behavior**

I’m considering the behavior of Fish with the `-c` option. After the command string, I think the remaining arguments become `$argv` in the script. So, for example, the syntax: 

```
fish -c 'opencode-forkworktree $argv' -- foo fix auth bug
```

will expand `$argv` to the arguments correctly. Now, using backticks, I’m thinking about this: 

```
!`fish -c 'opencode-forkworktree $argv' -- $ARGUMENTS 2>&1`
```

If `$ARGUMENTS` is substituted with "foo fix auth bug", it would provide `foo fix auth bug` as `$argv`. That’s great, but I need to consider how this works if the arguments include quotes.

**Clarifying ticket processing**

I'm looking at how the ticket system manages the title and issue key. It seems that when shifting `title=my-spike`, the issue key becomes TASK and is auto-generated. For prompts using `gwtt "$worktree_name" "$prompt" --no-edit`, I need to ensure the order is correct: issue key first, then title, and finally description. If the issue key isn't valid, it defaults to using the title. I might also want to consider how to handle prompts and support named help by checking if `gwtt` exists.

**Exploring function loading in Fish**

I’m considering whether to use `functions -q gwtt` or source files directly. In Fish shell, functions in the same path should work automatically, especially for non-interactive sessions using `-c`. My thinking is that Fish autoloads functions if the path includes `.config/fish/functions`, which is true since it’s stowed there. However, I'm pondering if Fish reads the config during non-interactive sessions. It seems like it generally does, except maybe with the `--no-config` flag. Also, I think I should call `gwt-ticket` instead of `gwtt`. I'll need to run some tests to verify Fish syntax validity.
I’m going to add a thin `/forkworktree` command and keep the real parsing in a Fish function so OpenCode only triggers the workflow and `gwtt` continues to own the tmux/worktree launch.

input.patchText:
*** Begin Patch
*** Add File: .config/fish/functions/opencode-forkworktree.fish
+function opencode-forkworktree --description "Launch a gwtt OpenCode worktree from an OpenCode slash command"
+    set -l show_help false
+    set -l worktree_name ""
+    set -l prompt_parts
+    set -l passthrough_args
+    set -l passthrough false
+
+    for arg in $argv
+        if $passthrough
+            set -a passthrough_args "$arg"
+            continue
+        end
+
+        switch $arg
+            case --help -h
+                set show_help true
+            case --
+                set passthrough true
+            case '*'
+                if test -z "$worktree_name"
+                    set worktree_name "$arg"
+                else
+                    set -a prompt_parts "$arg"
+                end
+        end
+    end
+
+    if $show_help; or test -z "$worktree_name"
+        echo "Usage: opencode-forkworktree <worktree-name> [prompt...] [-- gwtt-options...]"
+        echo ""
+        echo "Examples:"
+        echo "  opencode-forkworktree auth-spike investigate the auth refactor"
+        echo "  opencode-forkworktree ui-pass polish the dashboard -- --opencode-model anthropic/claude-opus-4-6"
+        return 0
+    end
+
+    set -l prompt (string join ' ' -- $prompt_parts)
+    if test -z "$prompt"
+        set prompt "Continue from the current repository state in a fresh OpenCode worktree named '$worktree_name'. First inspect git status, the current branch, and relevant context, then proceed independently."
+    end
+
+    gwtt "$worktree_name" "$prompt" --no-edit $passthrough_args
+end

state.title: Show forkworktree changes

content.summary:
(b1)

After the Pi/OpenCode parity gap analysis, user asked: "Why are subagents or model routing not configurable through Pi?" Answer given: Pi does not treat subagents or OpenCode-style policy routing as core config concepts. Pi supports model configuration/selection (`model`, enabled models, providers, `/model`, selection events), but not declarative task/agent-based routing like `.opencode/model-routing.json` (`plan -> claude-opus`, `execute -> gpt-5.5`, `batch -> qwen`, `triage -> haiku`). Pi also intentionally omits a native subagent abstraction with per-agent prompt/persona/tool permissions/model/background execution/auto-delegation/lifecycle hooks; such behavior would need an extension or external tmux/RPC orchestration.

User then asked: "Then tell me how i could set up my opencode so that i have a custom command like /forkworktree that forks but also creates that fork in a new tmux window with a worktree input similar to how we do gwtt <worktree-name>, would that be reasonable?" Read-only inspection was performed using the `customize-opencode` skill. Existing OpenCode command patterns were inspected: `.opencode/command/*.md` files use frontmatter with `description`, `agent`, `model`, `$ARGUMENTS`, and shell snippets using `!\`...\`` (e.g. `doctor.md`, `worktree-status.md`, `route.md`, `plan-work.md`). Existing `gwt-ticket.fish` was inspected and found to already implement the desired flow: creates worktree via `gwt-dev`, tmux session/window, OpenCode left pane, nvim top-right, terminal bottom-right; it defaults to OpenCode (`set -l use_codex true`), accepts `--opencode-model`, and launches OpenCode with `opencode --prompt "$initial_prompt"`. It names worktrees from non-ticket first arg by treating it as a title/slug, so `gwtt my-spike "prompt" --no-edit` creates branch/worktree/window named from `my-spike`. Existing `gwtt.fish` was read: it wraps `gwt-ticket`, foregrounds for help/status/queue/plan/verbose/desc-file stdin, otherwise writes a launcher script under `~/.claude/gwtt/logs`, runs it with `nohup fish`, disowns it, and prints `gwtt: launching ... in background`.

Recommendation given: implement `/forkworktree` as a thin OpenCode slash command plus a Fish wrapper that delegates to `gwtt`, not by recreating tmux/worktree logic inside OpenCode. Proposed user UX: `/forkworktree my-spike investigate auth refactor`, mapping to `gwtt my-spike "investigate auth refactor" --no-edit`. Clarified this is a manual slash-command automation entrypoint, not a lifecycle hook like `tool.execute.before`, but it can be the trigger into the existing orchestration. User asked: "Doesn't gwtt already default to opencode?" Answer: yes, no `--opencode` needed; `gwtt "$worktree_name" "$prompt" --no-edit` is sufficient, with optional `--opencode-model` only when desired. User then asked: "So basically I can use / commands in opencode to trigger literaly commands and this can be the hook that we use?" Answer: yes, OpenCode `/` commands can be user-triggered automation entrypoints that run shell snippets; use the slash command as a thin trigger and keep real parsing/behavior in a Fish function or script. Important distinction stated: slash command is manual trigger, not lifecycle hook; it can still call `gwtt`, which owns worktree creation, tmux window creation, OpenCode launch, nvim/terminal layout, hooks/Beads/Entire/plan/changelog setup.

parts.text: ▣ DCP | -190.8K removed, +14.5K summary — Compression #2

**Evaluating OpenCode setup**

I think the OpenCode configuration might be a bit tangled. The repo root could have a symlink to /.opencode, while global settings seem to point to /.config/opencode. The user wants to set up a custom command like /forkworktree, likely for their dotfiles repo. I wondered if the commands are global or just project-specific. I need to look into command directories and stow config, since it seems the changes should be in .opencode/command instead.

**Assessing command functionality**

I see that existing commands are in place, and I need to run tests to verify if quoting in the command shell snippet works properly. I used `!` with fish to run `opencode-forkworktree $argv`, but now I want to ensure that the substitution for `$ARGUMENTS` actually happens as expected in the shell code. I need to evaluate how OpenCode handles markdown shell command substitution, especially in this context. Let’s get clarity on this!
Build mode is active now. I already have the change in place and validated the targeted OpenCode test group; I’m doing one final worktree check before summarizing.

I’m thinking about whether to avoid using a certain tool related to cats because of potential command injection risks. I need to figure out how to mitigate this concern. I see that the existing debug command uses arguments only in the prompt, not the shell. Maybe I should look into worktree-status too?

23: !`if command -v bd >/dev/null 2>&1; then bd list --status=in_progress 2>/dev/null; else echo "bd not available"; fi`
24: 
25: Summarize the active work context briefly. Note any stale or stuck agents.

(End of file - total 25 lines)
</content>

parts.text:
**Exploring shell commands**

I’m thinking that command shell snippets might be executed by the shell after some templating. Maybe I could use base64, but I’m not sure if encoding in the shell is necessary. It might be more efficient to create a wrapper usage, like `/forkworktree <safe-slug>`, which could allow for prompt defaults without needing any user prompts in the shell. Exploring these options is definitely intriguing!

**Testing commands and files**

I need to run some tests, like using `fish -n ...`, and then `scripts/test-filter.sh opencode`, which has 124 passes. Also, I should run `fish -c 'opencode-forkworktree --help'`. I think it's important to mention the current git status, which might show something like "3 files changed." Keeping track of these details will help ensure everything is functioning as expected and that I'm not missing anything critical in the process.

Implemented `/forkworktree`.

Changed:
- Added `.opencode/command/forkworktree.md`
- Added `.config/fish/functions/opencode-forkworktree.fish`
- Updated `scripts/test-filter.sh` to validate both

Usage:
```text
/forkworktree auth-spike investigate auth refactor
```

This delegates to:
```fish
gwtt "auth-spike" "investigate auth refactor" --no-edit
```

Optional `gwtt` passthrough args:
```text
/forkworktree ui-pass polish dashboard -- --opencode-model anthropic/claude-opus-4-6
```

Validation passed:
- `fish -n .config/fish/functions/opencode-forkworktree.fish`
- `scripts/test-filter.sh opencode` -> `124` passed, `0` failed
- `fish -c 'opencode-forkworktree --help'`

Restart OpenCode before testing so it reloads the new command file.

When i run /forkworktree testblah it goes back into plan mode... the AI thinks avbout it and comes back with

I'm in Plan Mode (read-only), which prevents me from executing the 
gwtt testblah command. This conflicts with your request to launch and 
report on the worktree.

I have two options:

Exit Plan Mode → I can then run gwtt testblah and report the 
resulting tmux/worktree launch line
Stay in Plan Mode → I can only inspect current state (git status, 
branch, gwtt script structure) and explain what would happen
Which would you prefer? If you want me to proceed with the launch, 
I'll need to exit Plan Mode first.

**Evaluating command behavior**

I need to address the issue regarding the slash command that’s causing the plan mode to be read-only. It seems I might need to modify the command to use a non-plan agent, perhaps like general or precise? I’m also trying to understand the OpenCode command semantics since existing commands use the plan agent for read-only situations. If the model is refusing to run a shell snippet, I should check which agents are available and consider using the build mode, as it may have the necessary permissions.

**Diagnosing command execution issues**

It seems like the user's response suggests that the AI is attempting to execute the gwtt command, but the shell snippet might not be running. This raises the possibility that OpenCode doesn’t automatically execute shell snippets. I notice that the current command notation implies dynamic shell expansion, yet the plan agent might be blocking execution. The user mentions being in read-only mode, which hints that the command content was seen as a task to execute rather than expanding the shell. Could it be that the `!` syntax is causing confusion?

**Fixing command execution issues**

I'm exploring the current commands in the repository, wondering if OpenCode supports them. If it doesn't, that could be an issue. It seems that the /doctor command likely works due to tool permissions. To avoid being stuck in plan mode, I should set the agent to `build` or a custom `balanced` mode. Maybe I also need to tweak the command phrasing to say "Run this shell command exactly." If automatic shell expansion isn't happening, switching to build mode might resolve the issue, especially if it can run tools.

The command is selecting `agent: plan`, so OpenCode is doing exactly what the frontmatter tells it to do. I’m switching `/forkworktree` to an executable agent and tightening the prompt so it runs the wrapper instead of reasoning about it.

state.title: Show changed files

parts.text:
Fixed. `/forkworktree` was using `agent: plan`, which forced Plan Mode.

Changed it to:
```yaml
agent: build
```

Also tightened the prompt so it runs the wrapper and does not inspect/plan.

Added a regression test so it won’t drift back to plan mode.

Validation:
- `scripts/test-filter.sh opencode` -> `125` passed, `0` failed
- Fish syntax valid

Restart OpenCode, then try:
```text
/forkworktree testblah
```

[Image 1] It ran but opencode isn't open with the forked session in the left pane, thats what we'd want, so it replicates the fork functionality but in a new worktree following the gtww workflow, so forked worktree conversations are tracked on their own, does that make sense?

**Organizing work tasks**

I need to use a progress update before getting to work. I’m unsure about using TodoWrite since AGENTS suggest using bd, while the developer says use TodoWrite for complex tasks. However, CLAUDE advises using bd for all task tracking, which makes me think I should avoid TodoWrite. I’ll switch to build mode, inspect files, and check the relevant gwt-ticket. I might need to find occurrences of "no-edit" using grep, too, and read the launch section of the ticket for opencode.

That makes sense. The current wrapper is creating a normal `gwtt --no-edit` worktree, and `--no-edit` is the wrong default for this use case because it leaves you with the tmux layout but not the OpenCode agent session running in the left pane. I’m going to trace the `gwt-ticket` launch branch and adjust `/forkworktree` so it starts OpenCode in the left pane with a fork-context prompt.

2368:         '# Warm direnv/Nix in the interactive terminal pane before the first command.' \
2369:         "exec fish --init-command \"source $terminal_warmup_script_escaped\"" >$terminal_launch_script
2370:     chmod +x $terminal_launch_script
2371: 
2372:     if $use_devcon
2373:         # Build devcon up command. Reuse existing containers by default; opt into
2374:         # rebuilds only when mounts/features need to be refreshed.
2375:         set -l devcon_up_cmd "devcon claude -i $instance_name -E FORCE_AUTOUPDATE_PLUGINS=1 -E CLAUDE_CODE_ADDITIONAL_DIRECTORIES_CLAUDE_MD=1 -E CLAUDE_CODE_NO_FLICKER=0"
2376:         if $rebuild_devcon
2377:             set devcon_up_cmd "$devcon_up_cmd -r"
2378:         end
2379:         # Pass CLAUDE_CONFIG_DIR env var into container for subscription profile
2380:         if test -n "$sub_profile"
2381:             set devcon_up_cmd "$devcon_up_cmd -E CLAUDE_CONFIG_DIR=/home/node/.claude-$sub_profile"
2382:         end
2383:         # Pass provider env vars into container
2384:         if test -n "$provider_profile"
2385:             set -l conf_file "$HOME/.claude/providers/$provider_profile.conf"
2386:             while read -l line
2387:                 if string match -qr '^\s*#' "$line"; or string match -qr '^\s*$' "$line"
2388:                     continue
2389:                 end
2390:                 set -l kv (string replace -r '^\s*export\s+' '' "$line")
2391:                 set -l key (string split -m1 '=' "$kv")[1]
2392:                 set -l val (string split -m1 '=' "$kv")[2]
2393:                 set val (string trim -c "'" (string trim -c '"' "$val"))
2394:                 if test -n "$key" -a -n "$val"
2395:                     set devcon_up_cmd "$devcon_up_cmd -E $key=$val"
2396:                 end
2397:             end <"$conf_file"
2398:         end
2399:         if $bridge_mode
2400:             set devcon_up_cmd "$devcon_up_cmd -E CROSS_PROVIDER_BRIDGE=1"
2401:             if test -n "$bridge_iterations"
2402:                 set devcon_up_cmd "$devcon_up_cmd -E CROSS_PROVIDER_MAX_ITERATIONS=$bridge_iterations"
2403:             else
2404:                 set devcon_up_cmd "$devcon_up_cmd -E CROSS_PROVIDER_MAX_ITERATIONS=3"
2405:             end
2406:             if test -n "$bridge_providers"
2407:                 set devcon_up_cmd "$devcon_up_cmd -E CROSS_PROVIDER_ORDER=$bridge_providers"
2408:             end
2409:             if $bridge_verbose
2410:                 set devcon_up_cmd "$devcon_up_cmd -E CROSS_PROVIDER_VERBOSE=2"
2411:             end
2412:             if test -n "$bridge_review_mode"
2413:                 set devcon_up_cmd "$devcon_up_cmd -E CROSS_PROVIDER_MODE=$bridge_review_mode"
2414:             end
2415:             if test -n "$bridge_models"
2416:                 set devcon_up_cmd "$devcon_up_cmd -E CROSS_PROVIDER_MODELS=$bridge_models"
2417:             end
2418:             if test -n "$bridge_timeout"
2419:                 set devcon_up_cmd "$devcon_up_cmd -E CROSS_PROVIDER_TIMEOUT=$bridge_timeout"
2420:             end
2421:             if test -n "$bridge_log"
2422:                 set devcon_up_cmd "$devcon_up_cmd -E CROSS_PROVIDER_LOG=$bridge_log"
2423:             end
2424:             if $bridge_dry_run
2425:                 set devcon_up_cmd "$devcon_up_cmd -E CROSS_PROVIDER_DRY_RUN=1"
2426:             end
2427:             if test -n "$bridge_cooldown"
2428:                 set devcon_up_cmd "$devcon_up_cmd -E CROSS_PROVIDER_COOLDOWN=$bridge_cooldown"
2429:             end
2430:             if test -n "$bridge_profiles"
2431:                 set devcon_up_cmd "$devcon_up_cmd -E CROSS_PROVIDER_CLAUDE_PROFILES=$bridge_profiles"
2432:             end
2433:             if test -n "$bridge_codex_profiles"
2434:                 set devcon_up_cmd "$devcon_up_cmd -E CROSS_PROVIDER_CODEX_PROFILES=$bridge_codex_profiles"
2435:             end
2436:         end
2437:         set devcon_up_cmd "$devcon_up_cmd $worktree_path $repo_root"
2438:         for mount in $mounts
2439:             set devcon_up_cmd "$devcon_up_cmd $mount"
2440:         end
2441: 
2442:         # Config paths for devcontainer exec
2443:         set -l workspace "$HOME/.devcontainer/workspaces/$instance_name"
2444:         set -l config_file "$HOME/dotfiles/devcontainer/claude-code-plugins/.devcontainer/devcontainer.json"
2445:         set -l exec_cmd "devcontainer exec --config $config_file --workspace-folder $workspace"
2446: 
2447:         # Container-internal path for the launch script (via static devcontainer.json mount)
2448:         set -l container_launch_script "/devcontainer/env/launch-claude.fish"
2449: 
2450:         # Host-side wrapper for the Claude pane:
2451:         # 1. devcontainer exec runs Claude inside the container
2452:         # 2. Post-completion runs on host after Claude exits (has git, gh, etc.)
2453:         # 3. Falls back to interactive fish on failure so pane stays open for debugging
2454:         set -l claude_pane_script "$worktree_path/.claude/start-claude-pane.fish"
2455:         printf '%s\n' \
2456:             '#!/usr/bin/env fish' \
2457:             "cd $worktree_path" \
2458:             "$exec_cmd fish $container_launch_script" \
2459:             'set -l claude_exit $status' \
2460:             '' \
2461:             'if test $claude_exit -ne 0' \
2462:             "    echo 'Claude Code devcontainer exec failed (exit '\$claude_exit')'" \
2463:             "    echo 'Container: $instance_name'" \
2464:             "    echo 'Exec cmd: $exec_cmd'" \
2465:             "    echo 'Script: $container_launch_script'" \
2466:             '    exec fish' \
2467:             end \
2468:             '' \
2469:             '# Pane stays open for witness to use (conflict resolution, debugging)' \
2470:             'exec fish' >$claude_pane_script
2471:         chmod +x $claude_pane_script
2472: 
2473:         # Claude layout:
2474:         # ┌──────────────┬──────────────┐
2475:         # │ Claude Code  │ nvim CLAUDE.md│
2476:         # │ (left)       │              │
2477:         # │              ├──────────────┤
2478:         # │              │   terminal   │
2479:         # └──────────────┴──────────────┘
2480:         set -l setup_script "$worktree_path/.claude/setup-panes.fish"
2481:         set -l rename_script_devcon "$HOME/dotfiles/scripts/gwt-rename-session.sh"
2482:         if not test -x "$rename_script_devcon"
2483:             set rename_script_devcon "$HOME/dotfiles-rename/scripts/gwt-rename-session.sh"
2484:         end
2485:         printf '%s\n' \
2486:             '#!/usr/bin/env fish' \
2487:             '# Auto-generated by gwt-ticket - Claude left, editor+terminal right' \
2488:             "$devcon_up_cmd" \
2489:             'or begin' \
2490:             "    echo 'Devcontainer failed to start'" \
2491:             "    bash '$sandbox_script' default 2>/dev/null; or true" \
2492:             '    exit 1' \
2493:             end \
2494:             "set -l right_pane_id (tmux display-message -p '#{pane_id}')" \
2495:             "set -l claude_pane_id (tmux split-window -t \"\$right_pane_id\" -hb -p 35 -P -F '#{pane_id}' 'fish $claude_pane_script')" \
2496:             "tmux split-window -t \"\$right_pane_id\" -v -p 30 -c '$worktree_path' 'fish $terminal_launch_script'" \
2497:             "bash '$rename_script_devcon' \"\$claude_pane_id\" '$window_name' '$prompt_cmd_file' </dev/null >/dev/null 2>&1 &" \
2498:             disown \
2499:             "exec fish $nvim_launch_script" >$setup_script
2500:         chmod +x $setup_script
2501: 
2502:         # Start setup directly instead of typing into an interactive shell; this
2503:         # avoids Fish preexec/direnv work before the layout is created.
2504:         tmux respawn-pane -k -t "$session_name:$window_name" -c "$worktree_path" "fish $setup_script"
2505:         or begin
2506:             echo "Error: Failed to start devcontainer layout" >&2
2507:             builtin cd $_orig_pwd 2>/dev/null
2508:             return 1
2509:         end
2510:     else
2511:         if $use_codex
2512:             # OpenCode layout:
2513:             # ┌──────────────┬──────────────┐
2514:             # │  OpenCode    │ nvim         │
2515:             # │   (left)     ├──────────────┤
2516:             # │              │ terminal     │
2517:             # └──────────────┴──────────────┘
2518:             set -l right_pane_id (tmux display-message -p -t "$session_name:$window_name" '#{pane_id}')
2519:             set -l opencode_pane_id (tmux split-window -t "$right_pane_id" -hb -p 35 -P -F '#{pane_id}' "fish $launch_script")
2520:             if test -z "$opencode_pane_id"
2521:                 echo "Error: Failed to create OpenCode pane in $session_name:$window_name" >&2
2522:                 builtin cd $_orig_pwd 2>/dev/null

2525:             tmux split-window -t "$right_pane_id" -v -p 30 -c "$worktree_path" "fish $terminal_launch_script"
2526:             or echo "Warning: Failed to create terminal pane" >&2

2530:         else
2531:             # Claude layout:
2532:             # ┌──────────────┬──────────────┐

2539:             if test -z "$claude_pane_id"
2540:                 echo "Error: Failed to create Claude pane in $session_name:$window_name" >&2
2541:                 builtin cd $_orig_pwd 2>/dev/null

2547:             or echo "Warning: Failed to launch nvim pane" >&2

2549:             if not test -x "$rename_script"

2553:             disown

2052:     set -l prompt_cmd_file "$instance_env/prompt-cmd.txt"
2053:     set -l oneline_prompt (string replace -a \n ' ' -- "$prompt")
2054:

2056:     # so config.fish's interactive block is skipped — no direnv, no starship)
2057:     set -a _ls "cd $worktree_path"
2058: 
2059:     if $use_codex
2060:         # --- OpenCode harness ---
2061:         # Launch OpenCode on the host and seed the TUI prompt.
2062:         printf '%s' "$oneline_prompt" >$prompt_cmd_file
2063:         set -l opencode_cmd opencode
2064:         if test -n "$opencode_model"
2065:             set opencode_cmd "$opencode_cmd --model $opencode_model"
2066:         end
2067:         # Auth preflight is opt-in; `opencode auth list` is expensive enough to
2068:         # delay TUI startup, and OpenCode reports auth failures at runtime.
2069:         if $opencode_auth_preflight
2070:             set -a _ls 'set -gx OPENCODE_GWTT_AUTH_PREFLIGHT 1'
2071:         end
2072:         set -a _ls 'if test "$OPENCODE_GWTT_AUTH_PREFLIGHT" = 1'
2073:         set -a _ls "    set -l auth_list (opencode auth list 2>/dev/null | string collect)"
2074:         set -a _ls "    if not string match -iq '*$provider_display*' -- \$auth_list"
2075:         set -a _ls "        echo 'OpenCode is not authenticated for $provider_display. Starting login...'"
2076:         set -a _ls "        opencode auth login --provider $opencode_provider"
2077:         set -a _ls "        set auth_list (opencode auth list 2>/dev/null | string collect)"
2078:         set -a _ls "        if not string match -iq '*$provider_display*' -- \$auth_list"
2079:         set -a _ls "            echo 'OpenCode login for $provider_display did not complete.'"
2080:         set -a _ls '            exec fish'
2081:         set -a _ls '        end'
2082:         set -a _ls '    end'
2083:         set -a _ls end
2084:         # Usage limit preflight is opt-in: the public OpenAI API probe can
2085:         # disagree with OpenCode's ChatGPT subscription route. Runtime rotation
2086:         # still handles real 429/usage-limit errors after OpenCode starts.
2087:         set -a _ls 'if test "$OPENCODE_GWTT_USAGE_PREFLIGHT" = 1'
2088:         set -a _ls "    bash '$HOME/dotfiles/scripts/opencode/usage-check.sh' --quiet"
2089:         set -a _ls '    set -l _usage_exit $status'
2090:         set -a _ls '    if test $_usage_exit -eq 1'
2091:         set -a _ls "        echo 'OpenAI usage limit reached on current account.'"
2092:         set -a _ls '        if functions -q opencode-accounts'
2093:         set -a _ls '            opencode-accounts check-and-rotate'
2094:         set -a _ls '        else'
2095:         set -a _ls "            echo 'Run: opencode auth login --provider openai (to switch accounts)'"
2096:         set -a _ls "            opencode auth login --provider openai"
2097:         set -a _ls '        end'
2098:         set -a _ls '    end'
2099:         set -a _ls end
2100:         set -a _ls "set -l initial_prompt (cat '$prompt_cmd_file')"
2101:         set -a _ls "$opencode_cmd --prompt \"\$initial_prompt\""
2102:     else
2103:         # --- Claude harness: interactive with send-keys prompt delivery ---

2112:             printf '%s' "$slash_command \"$escaped_prompt\"" >$prompt_cmd_file
2113:         end

2116:             set claude_budget_flags "$claude_budget_flags --max-turns $max_turns"
2117:         end

1: function gwt-ticket --description "Execute ticket autonomously with OpenCode, nvim, and tmux worktrees"
2:     # Usage: gwt-ticket [issue-key] <title> <description> [options]
3:     #
4:     # Creates a git worktree, sets up a tmux window, and launches OpenCode by
5:     # default with nvim and terminal panes aligned to that worktree.
6:     #
7:     # If issue-key is omitted (first arg doesn't match ABC-123 pattern),
8:     # auto-generates TASK-YYYYMMDD-HHMMSS as the key.
9:     #
10:     # Options:
11:     #   --max N         Max iterations (default: 20)
12:     #   --max-turns N   Max agentic turns per Claude fallback session (budget cap)
13:     #   --max-budget N  Max API spend in USD before stopping (e.g., 5.00)
14:     #   --command C     Slash command to use (default: /ralph-loop:ralph-loop)
15:     #   --prompt-template F  File with custom prompt template
16:     #   --prompt-prefix P    Text to prepend to prompt
17:     #   --prompt-suffix S    Text to append to prompt
18:     #   --edit               Read description from per-repo gwtt-prompt.local.md (default)
19:     #   --no-edit            Use title as description instead of prompt file
20:     #   --desc-file FILE     Read description from file (- for stdin; avoids quote issues)
21:     #   --skill NAME [...]  Invoke skill(s) at prompt start
22:     #   --add-dir PATH [...] Additional directories for Claude fallback --add-dir
23:     #   --local         Use local Ollama model (default: qwen3-coder)
24:     #   --model MODEL   Use specific Ollama model (implies --local)
25:     #   --mount, -m     Additional mount (repeatable)
26:     #   --session S     Tmux session name (default: repo name)
27:     #   --devcon        Use devcontainer for isolation (default: local)
28:     #   --rebuild-devcon Rebuild devcontainer before launch (Claude fallback only)
29:     #   --sub NAME      Claude subscription profile (maps to ~/.claude-NAME config dir)
30:     #   --provider P    Claude fallback API provider profile (bedrock, vertex, foundry, gateway, or custom)
31:     #   --system S      Ticketing system: linear or jira
32:     #   --opencode      Use OpenCode mode (default)
33:     #   --claude        Use Claude Code fallback instead of OpenCode
34:     #   --opencode-model M  OpenCode model override
35:     #   --opencode-doctor Run OpenCode doctor preflight before launch
36:     #   --opencode-auth-check Check/login OpenCode auth before launch
37:     #   --codex         Legacy alias for --opencode
38:     #   --codex-model M Legacy OpenAI model alias for OpenCode
39:     #   --codex-profile P  Legacy compatibility flag; OpenCode auth comes from its own config
40:     #   --bridge        Enable cross-provider adversarial review flow
41:     #   --bridge-iterations N  Max bridge review cycles (default: 3)
42:     #   --bridge-mode M  Bridge review mode: review|redteam|steelman|assumptions
43:     #   --quiet, -q     Suppress verbose output (default, writes to .claude/gwt-ticket.log)
44:     #   --verbose, -v   Show full verbose output (overrides default quiet mode)
45:     #   --help, -h      Show help

Instructions from: /Users/shahe.islam/dotfiles-testingblah/.claude/AGENTS.md
# AGENTS.md - Claude Code Agent System Reference

Specialized agent system for Claude Code with domain, project-specific, and compact-output subagent files.

## Shell Reminder

- Fish is the primary shell for this machine.
- Do not suggest `unset` for interactive shell usage; use `set -e VAR` in Fish.
- `env -u VAR command` is acceptable for one-off commands started from Fish.
- If an example requires Bash or Zsh syntax, label it explicitly.

## Overview

Each agent is a **Claude Code subagent file** (`.claude/agents/*.md`) with YAML frontmatter defining name, description, tool access, model, and optional features. Claude auto-delegates to subagents based on their description fields. Each subagent runs in its own context window with a custom system prompt.

**Core Features**:
- **Subagent Files**: Markdown files with YAML frontmatter in `.claude/agents/`
- **Auto-Delegation**: Claude uses description fields to decide when to delegate
- **Tool Restrictions**: Each agent has specific tool access (read-only vs full)
- **Model Selection**: `haiku` for fast read-only agents, `inherit` for full capability
- **Persistent Memory**: `memory: project` on architect for cross-session learning
- **Background Mode**: `background: true` on test-runner for concurrent execution
- **Cost Control**: `maxTurns` on bounded agents (test-runner, dotfiles-doctor, mentor)
- **Skill Preloading**: `skills` field injects skill content at startup (shell-expert)
- **MCP Access**: `mcpServers: deepwiki` on architect and mentor for repo documentation
- **Lifecycle Hooks**: SubagentStart/SubagentStop events in settings.json for logging
- **Manual Override**: Use `--persona-[name]` flags for explicit control
- **Flag Integration**: Works with all thinking flags, MCP servers, and command categories

**Frontmatter Fields** (from [official docs](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/sub-agents)):

| Field | Required | Description |
|-------|----------|-------------|
| `name` | Yes | Agent identifier (must match filename) |
| `description` | Yes | When to delegate (Claude reads this) |
| `tools` | No | Comma-separated allowed tools |
| `disallowedTools` | No | Comma-separated blocked tools |
| `model` | No | `sonnet`, `opus`, `haiku`, or `inherit` (default) |
| `memory` | No | `user`, `project`, or `local` persistence scope |
| `background` | No | `true` for concurrent execution |
| `maxTurns` | No | Limit API round-trips (cost control) |
| `skills` | No | Comma-separated skills to preload |
| `mcpServers` | No | Comma-separated MCP servers to enable |
| `hooks` | No | Agent-scoped hooks (PreToolUse, PostToolUse, Stop) |
| `isolation` | No | `worktree` for isolated git worktree |
| `permissionMode` | No | Permission level override |

## Available Agents

### Technical Specialists

| Agent | Flag | Description | Primary Use Cases |
|-------|------|-------------|-------------------|
| [architect](agents/architect.md) | `--persona-architect` | Systems architecture specialist | System design, scalability, long-term planning |
| [frontend](agents/frontend.md) | `--persona-frontend` | UX specialist & accessibility advocate | UI components, responsive design, user experience |
| [backend](agents/backend.md) | `--persona-backend` | Reliability engineer & API specialist | Server development, APIs, data integrity |
| [security](agents/security.md) | `--persona-security` | Threat modeler & vulnerability specialist | Security audits, threat analysis, hardening |
| [performance](agents/performance.md) | `--persona-performance` | Optimization & bottleneck specialist | Performance tuning, metrics, optimization |

### Process & Quality Experts

| Agent | Flag | Description | Primary Use Cases |
|-------|------|-------------|-------------------|
| [analyzer](agents/analyzer.md) | `--persona-analyzer` | Root cause & investigation specialist | Debugging, troubleshooting, analysis |
| [qa](agents/qa.md) | `--persona-qa` | Quality advocate & testing specialist | Testing, validation, quality assurance |
| [refactorer](agents/refactorer.md) | `--persona-refactorer` | Code quality & technical debt manager | Code cleanup, refactoring, simplification |
| [devops](agents/devops.md) | `--persona-devops` | Infrastructure & automation specialist | CI/CD, deployment, infrastructure |
| [devops-security-auditor](agents/devops-security-auditor.md) | Special | DevOps security assessment specialist | Infrastructure security, container security |

### Knowledge & Communication

| Agent | Flag | Description | Primary Use Cases |
|-------|------|-------------|-------------------|
| [mentor](agents/mentor.md) | `--persona-mentor` | Educational & knowledge transfer specialist | Teaching, documentation, explanations |
| [scribe](agents/scribe.md) | `--persona-scribe=lang` | Documentation & localization specialist | Professional writing, documentation, translation |

### Project-Specific Agents

| Agent | Model | Extra Features | Primary Use Cases |
|-------|-------|----------------|-------------------|
| [shell-expert](agents/shell-expert.md) | inherit | `skills: fish-reload, dotfiles-sync` | Shell functions, scripts, Fish/Zsh parity |
| [test-runner](agents/test-runner.md) | haiku | `background: true`, `maxTurns: 10` | Running test suites, reporting results |
| [dotfiles-doctor](agents/dotfiles-doctor.md) | haiku | `maxTurns: 15` | Stow validation, tool checks, theme consistency |

### Compact Caveman Agents

Use these when the task has a known shape and the parent context needs operational signal without prose. Do not use them for open-ended architecture, security threat modeling, mentoring, or requirements discovery.

| Agent | Model | Extra Features | Primary Use Cases |
|-------|-------|----------------|-------------------|
| [caveman-investigator](agents/caveman-investigator.md) | haiku | `maxTurns: 8` | Root-cause evidence, file location, compact debug handoff |
| [caveman-reviewer](agents/caveman-reviewer.md) | inherit | `maxTurns: 10` | Findings-only review with severity, file:line, fix |
| [caveman-tester](agents/caveman-tester.md) | haiku | `background: true`, `maxTurns: 8` | Targeted validation receipts |
| [caveman-builder](agents/caveman-builder.md) | inherit | `maxTurns: 12` | Surgical execution of already-defined small changes |
| [caveman-checklist](agents/caveman-checklist.md) | haiku | `maxTurns: 6` | Terse execution checklist for already-scoped work |

## Agent Activation

### Manual Activation
Use flags to explicitly activate agents:
```bash
--persona-architect           # Activate architect agent
--persona-frontend --persona-qa  # Activate multiple agents
--persona-scribe=es          # Activate scribe with Spanish language
```

### Auto-Activation
Agents activate automatically based on:
- **Keywords**: Domain-specific terms trigger relevant agents
- **Context**: Project type and current task determine agent selection
- **Commands**: Specific commands have preferred agents
- **Complexity**: High complexity triggers analytical agents

### Command Integration
Commands work seamlessly with agents:
```bash
/build --persona-frontend     # Frontend-focused build
/analyze                       # Auto-activates analyzer, architect, security
/improve --persona-refactorer # Refactoring-focused improvements
/test --persona-qa            # QA-focused testing
```

## Cross-Agent Collaboration

Agents can work together for complex tasks:

### Common Collaboration Patterns
- **architect + performance**: System design with performance optimization
- **security + backend**: Secure server-side development
- **frontend + qa**: User-focused development with testing
- **mentor + scribe**: Educational content creation
- **analyzer + refactorer**: Root cause analysis with code improvement
- **devops + security**: Infrastructure automation with security

### Conflict Resolution
When multiple agents are active:
1. **Primary Agent**: Leads decision-making within domain
2. **Consulting Agents**: Provide specialized input
3. **Validation Agents**: Review decisions for quality
4. **Priority Matrix**: Resolves conflicts using agent hierarchies

## Flag Integration

All agents work with the complete flag system:

### Thinking Flags
- `--think`: Enhanced analysis (4K tokens)
- `--think-hard`: Deep analysis (10K tokens)
- `--ultrathink`: Maximum analysis (32K tokens)

### MCP Server Flags
- `--seq`: Sequential reasoning
- `--c7`: Context7 documentation
- `--magic`: UI component generation
- `--play`: Playwright testing

### Optimization Flags
- `--uc`: Ultra-compressed output
- `--validate`: Pre-operation validation
- `--safe-mode`: Conservative execution
- `--loop`: Iterative improvement

### Orchestration Flags
- `--wave-mode`: Multi-stage execution
- `--delegate`: Sub-agent delegation
- `--concurrency`: Parallel processing

## Agent Selection Guide

### By Task Type
- **Building**: frontend, backend, architect
- **Debugging**: analyzer, qa, performance
- **Security**: security, devops-security-auditor
- **Documentation**: scribe, mentor
- **Optimization**: performance, refactorer
- **Infrastructure**: devops, architect

### By Project Phase
- **Planning**: architect, analyzer
- **Implementation**: frontend, backend
- **Testing**: qa, performance
- **Deployment**: devops, security
- **Maintenance**: refactorer, analyzer

### By Complexity
- **Simple tasks**: Single specialized agent
- **Moderate tasks**: Primary + consulting agent
- **Complex tasks**: Multiple collaborative agents
- **Critical tasks**: Full agent team with validation

## Configuration

Agents respect all system configurations:
- Token limits and compression settings
- MCP server availability
- Resource management thresholds
- Quality gate requirements
- Validation cycles

## Best Practices

1. **Let auto-activation work**: The system usually selects the right agents
2. **Use explicit flags for precision**: Override when you need specific expertise
3. **Combine agents for complex tasks**: Multiple perspectives improve outcomes
4. **Trust agent expertise**: Each agent excels in their domain
5. **Monitor agent recommendations**: Agents provide domain-specific insights

## Quick Reference

```bash
# Single agent activation
--persona-frontend

# Multiple agents
--persona-architect --persona-security

# Agent with language
--persona-scribe=fr

# Agent with other flags
--persona-performance --think-hard --uc

# Command with agent
/build --persona-backend --validate
```

For detailed information about each agent, click on the agent name in the tables above to view their full specification.

## Agent Teams Integration

The persona-based agent system complements Claude Code's native **Agent Teams** feature (experimental). Agent Teams spawn separate Claude Code instances that communicate via peer-to-peer messaging, while personas operate within a single session.

### When to Use Each
| Scenario | Best Approach |
|----------|--------------|
| Single-session domain expertise | Personas (`--persona-*` flags) |
| Parallel same-repo collaboration | Agent Teams (teammates with shared task list) |
| Isolated multi-branch work | `gwt-parallel` (devcontainer + worktree per branch) |
| Autonomous ticket execution | `gwt-ticket` + OpenCode + nvim |

### Combining Personas with Agent Teams
Teammates spawned via Agent Teams load CLAUDE.md, so persona auto-activation works within each teammate's session. When creating teams, leverage this by assigning domain-specific context:
```
Spawn a teammate focused on security review (will auto-activate security persona)
Spawn a teammate for frontend components (will auto-activate frontend persona)
```


Instructions from: /Users/shahe.islam/dotfiles-testingblah/AGENTS.md
# Dotfiles Agent Guide

This file documents specific behaviors and constraints for AI agents working on this repository. Each rule addresses a concrete mistake agents repeatedly make.

## File 

- The tmux config is at `~/dotfiles/.tmux.conf` in the repo root. NEVER create `.config/tmux/tmux.conf` - it will conflict with the stow symlink.
- Neovim config is NOT in this repo. It lives at `~/neovim` (separate repository). Do not create or modify nvim configs here.
- Fish functions go in `.config/fish/functions/<name>.fish` as individual files, not inline in `config.fish`.
- All dotfiles are symlinked via GNU Stow from `~/dotfiles` to `~`. Do not manually create symlinks.
- Keep user-owned canonical data, reusable templates, and integration scripts in `~/dotfiles`; do not move the source of truth into third-party repos.
- Treat external repos (for example `~/career-ops`) as disposable runtime engines that can be recloned and repopulated from `~/dotfiles` via sync scripts.
- Prefer env-driven sync/generation into external repos over cross-repo symlinks so the workflow stays portable across machines and worktrees.

## Shell Scripts

- Fish is the primary shell. Write new shell functions in Fish syntax, not Bash.
- When modifying `config.fish`, it is 2800+ lines. Read the relevant section first, do not rewrite the entire file.
- Fish shell uses `set` not `export`. Fish uses `; and` not `&&`. Fish uses `test` not `[[ ]]`.
- For interactive Fish guidance, do not suggest `unset`; use `set -e VAR`.
- `env -u VAR command` is acceptable for one-off commands launched from Fish.
- If a Bash/Zsh-only snippet is necessary, label it explicitly as Bash/Zsh instead of implying it works as Fish.
- All new CLI tools must be added to BOTH `homebrew/Brewfile` AND `scripts/setup.sh`.
- PATH additions must go in both Fish config AND `scripts/setup.sh` (Zsh section) for compatibility.

## Package Management

- Use `bun`/`bunx` instead of `npm`/`npx`/`yarn`/`pnpm`. The `use_bun.py` hook will block npm commands.
- Homebrew packages go in `homebrew/Brewfile`, not installed via `brew install` in scripts.
- MCP servers must be configured in BOTH Claude Desktop config AND Claude Code CLI. Check both.

## Testing

- Run `scripts/smoke-test.sh` to validate basic dotfiles integrity.
- Run `scripts/validate-macos.sh` to check macOS-specific configurations.
- For Fish functions, test by sourcing the file: `source .config/fish/functions/<name>.fish`.
- There are no unit test frameworks for shell scripts - test by execution.
- Docker tests exist at `scripts/docker/` for Linux compatibility. Start Colima first.

## Available Tools for Agents

- `scripts/test-filter.sh [group]` - **Filtered test runner** (preferred for quick feedback). Run `--list` to see groups: fish, stow, claude, setup-syntax, brewfile, mcp, tmux, hooks, agents-md.
- `scripts/smoke-test.sh` - Quick validation of dotfiles setup.
- `scripts/validate-comprehensive.sh` - Full validation suite (slow).
- `scripts/test-lsp-inheritance.sh` - Verify LSP isolation works correctly.
- `scripts/pihole/test-pihole-setup.sh` - Test Pi-hole DNS setup.
- `scripts/devcontainer/test-claude-autologin.sh` - Test Claude autologin in devcontainers.
- `.claude/hooks/use_bun.py` - Enforces bun over npm (PreToolUse hook).
- `.claude/hooks/validate-bash.py` - Blocks dangerous bash commands (PreToolUse hook).
- `scripts/plan-validate.sh <plan.md>` - Validates plan markdown against DQS required sections (10 required, `--strict` for optional).

## Git Worktree Functions

- The `gwt-*` functions in `.config/fish/functions/` are the core worktree system.
- `gwt-dev.fish` creates worktrees with devcontainer isolation.
- `gwt-ticket.fish` is the autonomous ticket executor - read it before modifying.
- `devcon.fish` handles all devcontainer lifecycle - do not create separate container management.
- The devcon sandbox (`~/dotfiles/devcontainer/claude-code-plugins/`) is a built-in container config. Projects do NOT need their own `.devcontainer/` directory to use it. Never gate devcontainer usage on the project having `.devcontainer/`.
- All tmux panes in devcontainer windows must run inside the container (via `devcontainer exec`). When a container process exits (nvim, fish), the pane should re-enter the container shell, not drop to the host.

## Common Mistakes to Avoid

- Do not add emojis to commit messages.
- Do not reference AI tools in commit messages.
- Do not create files outside `~/dotfiles` (exception: `~/neovim`; exception: runtime logs/caches in `~/.claude/` — see below).
- Do not use `npx` for MCP commands in setup.sh - use `bunx`.
- Do not modify `.claude/settings.json` to manage **plugins** — use `claude plugin` commands.
- **Exception: Hooks** are configured directly in `.claude/settings.json` under the `"hooks"` key per [official docs](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/hooks). There is no CLI for hook registration. When wiring hooks, update settings.json in both `~/dotfiles/.claude/` (stow source) and verify `~/.claude/` (symlink target) reflects the change.
- **Exception: Runtime data** — Hook scripts may write runtime logs/caches to `~/.claude/hooks/logs/` and similar paths. This is expected behavior (same pattern as Beads `bd prime`, checkpoints, notification logs). These are runtime artifacts, not source configs, and must NOT be committed to the dotfiles repo.
- Do not create README.md files unless explicitly asked.
- Do not add Tokyo Night theme configs without checking all tools for consistency.

## Setup Script

- `scripts/setup.sh` is 1500+ lines with 11 phases. Read the relevant phase before editing.
- The setup script supports profiles: minimal, standard, comprehensive, dev, ops.
- New tool additions require: Brewfile entry + setup.sh phase + Fish/Zsh PATH config.
- Test setup changes with `--dry-run` flag first.

## When NOT to Use Agents for This Repo


- tmux plugin installation: TPM handles this, just add to `.tmux.conf`.
- Brewfile sorting/organization: maintain the existing grouping structure.
- LazyVim plugin config: this lives in `~/neovim`, not here.


## Landing the Plane (Session Completion)

**When ending a work session**, you MUST complete ALL steps below. Work is NOT complete until `git push` succeeds.

**MANDATORY WORKFLOW:**

1. **File issues for remaining work** - Create issues for anything that needs follow-up
2. **Run quality gates** (if code changed) - Tests, linters, builds
3. **Update issue status** - Close finished work, update in-progress items
4. **PUSH TO REMOTE** - This is MANDATORY:
   ```bash
   git pull --rebase
   git push
   git status  # MUST show "up to date with origin"
   ```
5. **Clean up** - Clear stashes, prune remote branches
6. **Verify** - All changes committed AND pushed
7. **Hand off** - Provide context for next session

**CRITICAL RULES:**
- Work is NOT complete until `git push` succeeds
- NEVER stop before pushing - that leaves work stranded locally
- NEVER say "ready to push when you are" - YOU must push
- If push fails, resolve and retry until it succeeds

</system-reminder>

cd /Users/shahe.islam/dotfiles-testingblah
if test "$OPENCODE_GWTT_AUTH_PREFLIGHT" = 1
    set -l auth_list (opencode auth list 2>/dev/null | string collect)
    if not string match -iq '*OpenAI*' -- $auth_list
        echo 'OpenCode is not authenticated for OpenAI. Starting login...'
        opencode auth login --provider openai
        set auth_list (opencode auth list 2>/dev/null | string collect)
        if not string match -iq '*OpenAI*' -- $auth_list
            echo 'OpenCode login for OpenAI did not complete.'
            exec fish
        end
    end
end
if test "$OPENCODE_GWTT_USAGE_PREFLIGHT" = 1
    bash '/Users/shahe.islam/dotfiles/scripts/opencode/usage-check.sh' --quiet
    set -l _usage_exit $status
    if test $_usage_exit -eq 1

11: 2. Understand the existing codebase first

16: 7. When complete, output TICKET_TASK_COMPLETE

20: 
21:

23: 
24: ALWAYS decompose multi-step tasks into subtasks. Only skip decomposition for true single-file, single-change fixes.
25: 
26: Workflow:
27: 1. Find the parent bead: bd list --status=open
28: 2. Create subtasks linked to parent:
29:    bd create --title='SUBTASK_TITLE' --description='WHY_AND_WHAT' --type=task --priority=2 --parent PARENT_BEAD_ID
30:    Use 'bd dep add CHILD_ID BLOCKER_ID' if ordering matters.
31: 3. Work each subtask:
32:    bd update BEAD_ID --status=in_progress → code → run tests if applicable → commit → bd close BEAD_ID
33:    Hooks enforce syntax and formatting on commit. Focus on testing and bead lifecycle.
34: 4. Between subtasks: bd ready (shows what is unblocked next)
35: 
36: Subtask state survives context compaction via bd prime.
37: 
38: REQUIRED BEHAVIORS:
39: - ALWAYS use 'bd comments add ID "why I chose approach X over Y"' for decisions and trade-offs
40: - ALWAYS use 'bd q "discovered issue"' to capture issues found during work (silent, returns only ID)
41: - ALWAYS use 'bd update ID --append-notes "finding or decision"' to record findings
42: - ALWAYS use 'bd search "keyword"' to check for related beads before creating new ones
43: - Close multiple: bd close ID1 ID2 ID3 (batch close is more efficient)
44: - Check blocked: bd blocked (see what needs unblocking)
45: - Store metadata: bd kv set key value (persistent across compactions)
46: 
47: CODEX DELEGATION — For independent subtasks, consider using /codex:rescue to delegate work to Codex in background:
48: - Use when a subtask is self-contained and doesn't depend on your current work
49: - Run: /codex:rescue --background 'Complete subtask: TITLE. Details: DESCRIPTION'
50: - Check progress: /codex:status
51: - Get results: /codex:result JOB_ID
52: Only delegate when you have 3+ subtasks AND the subtask is truly independent.
53: 
54: LIVING PLAN — A plan file exists at .plan.md for this session.
55: This file persists across context compactions and session restarts.
56: 
57: UPDATE .plan.md at these points:
58: - After initial codebase investigation (fill in Approach section)
59: - After completing each major subtask (update Progress + Current State)
60: - After a subagent returns results (capture key findings in Current State)
61: - Before any natural stopping point (update Next Steps)
62: - When making important decisions (add to Key Decisions)
63: - When an approach fails or a dead end is found (add to Failed Approaches with WHY it failed)
64: - When a command produces valuable results or solves a problem (add to Useful Commands)
65: - When closing a bead (update Progress checklist to match bead status)
66: 
67: DEAD-END PREVENTION: Before trying a new approach, check the Failed Approaches section.
68: If an approach is listed there, do NOT retry it — find an alternative.
69: When recording a failed approach, always include WHY it failed so future sessions understand.
70: 
71: Only save commands that are genuinely useful — not routine ls/git status calls.
72: ALWAYS use Edit (not Write) when updating .plan.md to avoid clobbering external edits.
73: The plan is your persistent memory. Hooks will re-inject it after compaction.

(End of file - total 73 lines)
</content>
